tEn: Inside Anthropic's Unprecedented Growth: From $1B to $19B ARR in 14 Months
tZh: 揭秘 Anthropic 史无前例的增长：14 个月内从 10 亿到 190 亿美元 ARR
sEn: Amol Avaisary, Head of Growth at Anthropic, shares how the company achieved the fastest growth in history, from $1B to $19B ARR in 14 months, and how he landed the role via a cold email to Mike Krieger.
sZh: Anthropic 增长负责人 Amol Avasare 分享公司如何实现史上最快增长——14 个月内从 10 亿到 190 亿美元 ARR，以及他如何通过一封冷邮件获得这个职位。

consensus.en: Anthropic's growth is driven by exponential model improvements, not just growth team optimizations.
consensus.zh: Anthropic 的增长主要源于模型的指数级进步，而非单纯的增长团队优化。

consensus.en: Activation is critical for AI products; adding the right friction helps users understand product value.
consensus.zh: 激活对 AI 产品至关重要；增加适当的摩擦有助于用户理解产品价值。

consensus.en: Growth teams should shift toward larger bets when product value grows exponentially.
consensus.zh: 当产品价值呈指数增长时，增长团队应转向更大规模的投入。

consensus.en: AI can automate growth experimentation, but cross-functional stakeholder management still needs humans.
consensus.zh: AI 可以自动化增长实验，但跨职能利益相关者管理仍需人类。

consensus.en: Engineers gain the most leverage from AI tools like Claude Code, straining PM and design roles.
consensus.zh: 工程师从 Claude Code 等 AI 工具中获得最大杠杆，给 PM 和设计角色带来压力。

consensus.en: Anthropic's focus on coding and B2B, driven by leadership, has been key to its success.
consensus.zh: Anthropic 专注于编码和企业级市场，由领导层推动，是其成功的关键。

contrarian.en: Adding friction in onboarding often improves conversion by helping users find the right product.
contrarian.zh: 在 onboarding 中增加摩擦通常能通过帮助用户找到合适产品来提高转化率。

contrarian.en: PMs should focus on upskilling engineers as mini-PMs rather than shipping features themselves.
contrarian.zh: PM 应专注于培养工程师成为迷你 PM，而非亲自交付功能。

contrarian.en: AI can identify misalignment across teams, reducing coordination toil before it causes issues.
contrarian.zh: AI 可以识别团队间的错位，在问题出现前减少协调负担。

contrarian.en: Growth teams should be comfortable leaving money on the table to prioritize safety and brand.
contrarian.zh: 增长团队应乐于放弃短期收益，优先考虑安全和品牌。

contrarian.en: Anthropic's safety focus is a long-term competitive advantage, not a hindrance to growth.
contrarian.zh: Anthropic 对安全的关注是长期竞争优势，而非增长障碍。

contrarian.en: A traumatic brain injury can improve product management skills through enforced constraints and mindfulness.
contrarian.zh: 创伤性脑损伤通过强制约束和正念，可以提升产品管理技能。

transcript.sec: Anthropic's unprecedented growth

turns.en: A lot of companies claim to be the fastest-growing companies of all time, and Anthropic actually is. You guys were at a billion ARR at the start of 2025. The last number I've seen is 19 billion ARR. That's 1 to 19 billion dollars in 14 months.
turns.zh: 很多公司都声称自己是史上增长最快的公司，而 Anthropic 确实是。你们在 2025 年初的 ARR 是 10 亿美元。我看到的最后一个数字是 190 亿美元。也就是说，14 个月内从 10 亿增长到 190 亿美元。

turns.en: Historically, we were very much the smallest, least well-funded player in this space. We didn't have the free cash flow or the distribution of a Meta or Google. We didn't have the first-mover advantage of an OpenAI. It's a complete miracle that we've gotten to the stage that we have.
turns.zh: 从历史上看，我们一直是这个领域最小、资金最少的玩家。我们没有 Meta 或 Google 那样的自由现金流或分发渠道，也没有 OpenAI 的先发优势。我们能走到今天这一步，完全是个奇迹。

turns.en: Give us just a glimpse of what it's like to be leading growth inside of Anthropic.
turns.zh: 请给我们简单介绍一下在 Anthropic 领导增长是什么感觉。

turns.en: It's the hottest job I've had in my life. You come into Anthropic, you need to understand that 50, 60, 70% of how you operate in the past, just throw it out the door.
turns.zh: 这是我一生中最热门的职位。你来到 Anthropic，需要明白过去 50%、60%、70% 的运作方式，直接扔掉。

turns.en: One of the cleverest growth moves you all made was this idea of importing memory from ChatGPT.
turns.zh: 你们做的最聪明的增长举措之一就是从 ChatGPT 导入记忆这个想法。

turns.en: Activation is a really big challenge in AI. We are starting to look at how do we automate growth? Our growth platform team is driving this effort called Cache, which is Claude Accelerates Sustainable hypergrowth. How can we use Claude to automate growth experimentation? And it's delivering results.
turns.zh: 激活在 AI 中是一个非常大的挑战。我们开始研究如何自动化增长？我们的增长平台团队正在推动一个名为 Cache 的项目，即 Claude 加速可持续超增长。我们如何利用 Claude 来自动化增长实验？它正在带来成果。

turns.en: You're basically living in the future.
turns.zh: 你基本上就生活在未来。

turns.en: We always talk about the exponential. The product value that we will deliver in 2 years' time is probably like a thousand X what it is today. The funniest thing is I've noticed internally, linear charts are just not cool. Everything is log-linear, so show me it log-linear scale.
turns.zh: 我们总是谈论指数增长。两年后我们将交付的产品价值可能是今天的一千倍。最有趣的是，我注意到在公司内部，线性图表根本不酷。一切都是对数线性的，所以请用对数线性标度展示给我看。

transcript.sec: How Amol got the role at Anthropic

turns.en: Today, my guest is Amol Avasare. Amol is head of growth at Anthropic, which is on the most unprecedented growth run in history. In the past 14 months, they grew from 1 billion to over 19 billion in annual recurring revenue. Just in the past few months, their revenue doubled. They've been growing 10X year-over-year. This is unheard of at this scale. By the time this episode comes out, their revenue will be even higher. To put this scale in perspective, companies like Atlassian and Palantir and Snowflake, which have been around for 15 to 20 years, each do something like 4 and 1/2 to 6 billion in ARR. Anthropic is adding this much ARR every few months. And if that isn't interesting enough to you, Amol, who leads growth at Anthropic, is an incredible human. He previously led growth at Mercury and Masterclass. Before that, he was a founder and an investment banker. And most interestingly, something that most people don't know about him is that Amol suffered a severe brain injury. He had to spend 9 months relearning how to walk and work and just not be nauseous all the time. He shared this story in a guest post in my newsletter a number of years ago. We actually chat about this during the conversation. These are my favorite kind of conversations because Amol and his team are living in the future, and he's come to tell us where things are heading and what's going to change. And in this episode, Amol shares an unprecedented look at how a company like Anthropic operates and grows, including how they think about growth, what parts of the job they've automated, the future of the product and growth roles, how Amol got the job first place by cold emailing Mike Krieger, and so much more. Amol is wonderful, and just try to count the number of times that he blew my mind during this conversation. Before we get into it, don't forget to check out Lenny's Product Pass.com for an incredible set of deals available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers. With that, I bring you Amol Avasare. Amol, thank you so much for being here, and welcome to the podcast.
turns.zh: 今天我的嘉宾是 Amol Avasare。Amol 是 Anthropic 的增长负责人，该公司正经历历史上最前所未有的增长。在过去 14 个月里，他们的年经常性收入从 10 亿美元增长到超过 190 亿美元。就在过去几个月里，他们的收入翻了一番。他们一直以每年 10 倍的速度增长。这在如此规模上是闻所未闻的。到本期节目发布时，他们的收入还会更高。为了更直观地理解这个规模，像 Atlassian、Palantir 和 Snowflake 这些已经存在了 15 到 20 年的公司，各自的 ARR 大约在 45 亿到 60 亿美元之间。而 Anthropic 每隔几个月就能增加这么多 ARR。如果这还不够有趣，那么领导 Anthropic 增长的 Amol 是一个了不起的人。他之前曾在 Mercury 和 Masterclass 领导增长。在此之前，他是一名创始人和投资银行家。最有趣的是，大多数人不了解他的一点是，Amol 曾遭受严重的脑损伤。他花了 9 个月时间重新学习如何走路、工作，以及如何不一直感到恶心。他几年前在我的 newsletter 的一篇客座文章中分享了这个故事。我们实际上在对话中聊到了这一点。这是我最喜欢的一类对话，因为 Amol 和他的团队生活在未来，他来告诉我们事情的发展方向以及将会发生什么变化。在本期节目中，Amol 前所未有地分享了像 Anthropic 这样的公司如何运作和增长，包括他们如何看待增长、他们自动化了哪些工作、产品和增长角色的未来、Amol 如何通过给 Mike Krieger 发冷邮件获得这份工作，以及更多。Amol 非常棒，试着数数在这次对话中他让我震惊了多少次。在开始之前，别忘了查看 Lenny's Product Pass.com，那里有专门为 Lenny 的 newsletter 订阅者提供的超值优惠。话不多说，有请 Amol Avasare。Amol，非常感谢你来到这里，欢迎来到播客。

turns.en: Glad to be here.
turns.zh: 很高兴来到这里。

turns.en: Head of growth at Anthropic. No big deal. I've had a lot of people come on this podcast from companies that claim to be the fastest growing companies of all time, and Anthropic actually is if you look at the trajectory. I just have some of the numbers here just so people understand how absurd this is. So, you guys were at a billion ARR at the start of 2025, then hit something like 4 billion mid-2025, then 9 billion ARR at the end of 2025. And the last number I've seen is you guys are at 19 billion ARR. Which uh just to put a couple pieces of context here. One is that's from one to 19 billion dollars in 14 months. Um I have so many questions. First of all, the story of how you actually landed this role is really interesting. Talk about how you how you got this role.
turns.zh: Anthropic 的增长负责人，没什么大不了的。很多公司都声称自己是史上增长最快的公司，而如果你看增长轨迹，Anthropic 确实是。我这里有一些数字，让大家明白这有多离谱。你们在 2025 年初的 ARR 是 10 亿美元，然后在 2025 年中达到大约 40 亿美元，2025 年底达到 90 亿美元。我看到的最后一个数字是你们达到了 190 亿美元 ARR。这里提供一些背景信息：一是从 10 亿到 190 亿美元只用了 14 个月。我有很多问题。首先，你实际上是如何得到这个角色的故事非常有趣。谈谈你是怎么得到这个职位的吧。

turns.en: Yeah, it's a little unorthodox. So, it's funny in when I when I did my onboarding they they walked through what percentage of the cohort came through referrals, what percentage came through applying on the website, what percentage came through uh sourcing, and I was on none of those. And uh I was like, "Okay, this is interesting." Um basically the the way that I I got to Anthropic was that I was actually a user of Claude, and I was using a lot. I was like, "Man, these guys like great product, great company, but they they really they obviously don't have a growth team." And uh what I did was I just sent Mike Krieger a cold email. He was the chief product officer. I sent him a cold email saying like, "Hey, love what you guys do, love the product. I think you guys badly need a growth team. Want to chat?" And uh I didn't expect he would respond. And uh you know, he responds and says, "Hey, yeah, I'm interested. Let's let's talk." And it's funny, I didn't know I mean, I wouldn't have known they were not hiring for a growth team. There were no growth team roles listed. But they were just at that time starting to think about hiring a growth team. So, it was very good timing, but yeah, one one spoke to spoke to Mike, and and one thing led to another. He said I'm the only PM that he's hired from from cold email. And I I feel very lucky that he decided to respond to my email.
turns.zh: 是的，有点非正统。有趣的是，在我入职时，他们介绍了有多少比例的人是通过推荐、网站申请、猎头来源进来的，而我哪个都不是。我当时想，“好吧，这挺有意思的。”基本上，我进入 Anthropic 的方式是，我实际上是 Claude 的用户，而且用得很多。我想，“天哪，这些人产品好，公司好，但他们显然没有增长团队。”于是我就给 Mike Krieger 发了一封冷邮件。他是首席产品官。我发了一封冷邮件说：“嘿，喜欢你们做的事，喜欢这个产品。我觉得你们急需一个增长团队。想聊聊吗？”我没想到他会回复。然后他回复说：“嘿，是的，我有兴趣。我们聊聊吧。”有趣的是，我不知道他们当时并没有在招聘增长团队。没有任何增长团队的职位列出。但他们正好在那时开始考虑招聘增长团队。所以时机非常好。总之，我和 Mike 聊了聊，然后事情就顺理成章了。他说我是他唯一一个通过冷邮件招聘的 PM。我觉得非常幸运他决定回复我的邮件。

turns.en: I did not know the story. That is that is another uh absurd fact. Uh clearly you're good at cold email. What did you do in this cold email to get his attention?
turns.zh: 我之前不知道这个故事。这又是一个离谱的事实。显然你很擅长冷邮件。你在那封冷邮件里做了什么来吸引他的注意？

turns.en: I would say like I've basically perfected cold email over the years. So, I was a when I was a founder, I I had to get really, really good at this. So, I I sent a lot of cold emails out and just honed the the the subject lines and message and and the tone. And and so, basically, I have in the subject line, the first thing is like from a conversion standpoint, someone sees the email, they need to click on it. And so, I have a copy that I've tested that is like very, very high open rate. And so,
turns.zh: 我想说，我基本上多年来已经完善了冷邮件。当我还是创始人时，我不得不变得非常擅长这个。我发了很多冷邮件，不断打磨主题行、信息和语气。基本上，在主题行里，从转化角度看，第一件事是有人看到邮件，他们需要点击它。所以我有一个经过测试的文案，打开率非常高。

turns.en: Wait, what is this copy? Or is this a secret?
turns.zh: 等等，这个文案是什么？还是说这是个秘密？

turns.en: It's a secret. I think
turns.zh: 这是个秘密。我觉得

turns.en: Okay, we'll keep it We'll kind of keep
turns.zh: 好吧，我们保密。

turns.en: Yeah, it's it's a secret. Um so, that's one, getting them to open. I think the second is then the tactics of You need to understand like where are people getting outreach. And if you If everyone's getting outreach in one area and then uh you reach out to them there, then then you're not going to get as high of a response rate.
turns.zh: 是的，这是个秘密。嗯，所以第一点是让他们打开邮件。我认为第二点是策略：你需要了解人们在哪里收到 outreach。如果每个人都在一个领域收到 outreach，然后你在那里联系他们，那么你的回复率就不会那么高。

transcript.sec: Cold emailing and persistence

turns.en: So, you can think about like LinkedIn, you can think about like work email. These are things that everyone is emailing. So, there's ways to get people's personal emails out there. And so, like that's one thing that I did. And so, okay, I've got his personal email, I know the copy that works. And then it's just keeping it very short on here's who I am, here's why I'd be a good fit, and we should chat. And you know, these things typically don't work. And then you should always follow up a few times. I think my rule of thumb is like if I really care about it, I should just keep reaching out to them until they tell me like, "Please stop." And so, I would have kept doing that, but he responded the first time. It makes sense that a talented growth person would be very good at cold email and getting people's attention. So, that's almost like an interview step is just did I want to read this email?
turns.zh: 所以，你可以想想 LinkedIn，想想工作邮箱。这些都是大家都会发邮件的地方。所以，有办法找到人们的个人邮箱。这就是我做过的一件事。然后，好了，我有了他的个人邮箱，我知道有效的文案。然后就是保持简短：我是谁，为什么我适合，我们应该聊聊。你知道，这些事情通常不管用。然后你应该跟进几次。我的经验法则是，如果我真的在乎，我就应该一直联系他们，直到他们告诉我“请停止”。所以，我会一直这么做，但他第一次就回复了。一个优秀增长人才很擅长冷邮件和吸引注意力，这说得通。所以，这几乎就是一个面试步骤：我想不想读这封邮件？

transcript.sec: Leading growth at Anthropic

turns.en: Okay. So, give us just like a glimpse of what it's like to be leading growth inside of Anthropic right now, the most by far fastest growing company in history. Just what is it like?
turns.zh: 好的。那么，给我们一个 glimpse，看看在 Anthropic 领导增长是什么感觉，这是迄今为止历史上增长最快的公司。到底是什么样的？

turns.en: Yeah, I'd say it's very much a company-wide effort, right? So, like I guess we are the growth team. We have done great. I think we've driven a lot of impact, but honestly, man, we can't claim too much credit for the success of the company. We as Anthropic are really a model company and an intelligence company first and foremost. And so, the lion's share of what has driven our success is our research team. We have I think the best research team in the world. We have great teams on inference and compute. And then there's many other teams like Claude Code, Dodo Marketing, etc. who I think deserve much more credit than us. And I think you're zooming out going to some of what you said earlier that the growth trajectory has just been insane. That 10x year-on-year revenue growth trend has been there since the beginning. I think 2023 was zero to 100 mil. 2024 was 100 to one. Last year was one to roughly 10. And then like I look back to when I joined in 2024, revenue was in the hundreds of millions. And just that trajectory to the end of 2024 and 2025 like week two of when I joined, we're going into 2025 revenue planning and we have these like base case and aggressive case scenarios and Darius pushing the aggressive case scenario and people are freaking out being like how the hell are we going to hit that and Darius like I think we can actually go much higher than that. And I'm coming in like this place is crazy like there's absolutely no way. And that happened, right? And then you get to the end of 2025 and it's like okay, law of large numbers, there's going to be a pretty big slow down here based on your baseline rate of 10 billion, how are you going to keep growing at this rate? And like it just has not slowed down and you know, these numbers are public. The 19 billion number you quoted is from the end of Feb. So that is also out of date. And it's look, it's absolutely insane. Like the funniest thing is something that I'm noticing internally is like linear charts are just not cool. Like no one cares about linear charts. Everything is log linear. I'll show me it log linear scale and that's the scale we think and I think overall we're just, you know, really hanging on by the seat of our pants and we're trying to manage the growth and do the best that we can for our users.
turns.zh: 是的，我想说这很大程度上是全公司的努力，对吧？所以，我想我们是增长团队。我们做得很好。我认为我们带来了很多影响，但老实说，我们不能把公司的成功归功于自己太多。Anthropic 首先是一家模型公司和智能公司。所以，推动我们成功的主要部分是我们的研究团队。我们拥有我认为世界上最好的研究团队。我们在推理和算力方面有很棒的团队。然后还有很多其他团队，比如 Claude Code、Dodo Marketing 等，我认为他们比我们更值得称赞。我想你放大来看，就像你之前说的，增长轨迹简直疯狂。10 倍年同比增长趋势从一开始就存在。我认为 2023 年是从 0 到 1 亿。2024 年是从 1 亿到 10 亿。去年是从 10 亿到大约 100 亿。然后，我回想我 2024 年加入时，收入是数亿。然后到 2024 年底和 2025 年，我加入的第二周，我们进入 2025 年收入规划，我们有基准情况和激进情况方案，Darius 推动激进方案，人们吓坏了，说我们到底怎么才能达到那个目标，而 Darius 说我认为我们实际上可以比那高得多。我进来时觉得这地方疯了，绝对不可能。然后那发生了，对吧？然后到了 2025 年底，你会想，好吧，大数定律，基于 100 亿的基准率，这里会有相当大的放缓，你怎么能保持这个速度增长？但它就是没有放缓，你知道，这些数字是公开的。你引用的 190 亿是 2 月底的数据。所以那也已经过时了。而且，这绝对疯狂。最有趣的是我在内部注意到的一件事：线性图表根本不酷。没人在乎线性图表。一切都是对数线性。给我看对数线性尺度，那是我们思考的尺度。我认为总的来说，我们只是勉强支撑，努力管理增长，为我们的用户做到最好。

transcript.sec: Role of the growth team

turns.en: I was talking to somebody at Anthropic about you and they said that basically anytime they want something to grow, they ask you to help and it works. So you talked about just like things are magical and amazing and like Claude and all the tools you all built are amazing innately and that's a big part of the reason they grow. I think many people listening to this will be like what do you act even do a mole with like a magical micro god that just can do anything for you? Why do we need a growth person? What do you even do? Talk about just the stuff that you focus on and maybe like a couple of the wins that your team has shipped that has helped accelerate growth.
turns.zh: 我和 Anthropic 的某个人聊到你，他们说基本上任何时候他们想让某样东西增长，就会请你帮忙，而且很有效。所以你刚才说事情很神奇、很棒，Claude 和你们构建的所有工具天生就很棒，这是它们增长的重要原因。我想很多听众会想，你实际上做什么？就像一个神奇的微神，什么都能为你做？为什么我们需要一个增长人员？你到底做什么？谈谈你关注的事情，也许还有你的团队推出的一些有助于加速增长的胜利。

turns.en: I would say like they're not fully wrong, right? Like we're very lucky to have the best models in the world. We're very lucky to have products like Claude Code and co-work. It suddenly makes life a lot easier. Having said that, I would say this is like the hardest job I've had in my life and that's, you know, having been a founder, having been an investment banker and other things like that. It's tough and if I look at what do we do as a growth team here? I think it's ultimately the same categories of things that you would think about at a normal company. So, we care about acquisition, how are we getting more people in the door, there are the intent of the people coming through the door, we care about activation, the sign-up flow, funneling people to the right products, making them successful. You know, we care about things like monetization, free to paid conversion, pricing and packaging, all of that stuff that the categories of work is the same. I think then the probably the big differences is I would say that like roughly 70% of what I spend my time on is what we internally refer to as success disasters and that is where things have gone so well that other things are breaking now and I think anyone who's worked at companies that have gone through rapid growth, like Facebook or Uber or DoorDash early on, like they understand this viscerally.
turns.zh: 我想说他们并不完全错，对吧？我们非常幸运拥有世界上最好的模型。我们非常幸运拥有像 Claude Code 和 co-work 这样的产品。这突然让生活变得容易多了。话虽如此，我想说这是我一生中最难的工作，你知道，我当过创始人，当过投资银行家，还有其他类似经历。这很艰难，如果我看看我们作为增长团队在这里做什么？我认为最终和你在正常公司会考虑的类别是一样的。所以，我们关心获客，如何让更多人进门，进门的人的意图，我们关心激活，注册流程，将人们引导到正确的产品，让他们成功。你知道，我们关心变现、免费到付费转化、定价和包装等事情，所有这些工作类别都是一样的。我认为最大的不同可能是，我大约 70% 的时间花在我们内部称为“成功灾难”的事情上，那就是事情进展得太顺利，以至于其他东西现在正在崩溃。我认为任何在经历过快速增长的公司工作过的人，比如早期的 Facebook、Uber 或 DoorDash，他们都能切身理解这一点。

transcript.sec: Challenges of Scaling and Time Allocation

turns.en: Where scaling this much just brings a lot of challenges. If you think about each of those categories on acquisition, on activation, on monetization, there's just a ton of firefighting, jumping from one urgent thing to another and it's often extremely painful. And it's funny because you look at all the charts, all the charts are green, fully up and to the right, and everyone's just like... it can be quite tough emotionally still. So, you need to sort of step back and just realize that we're very lucky to have these problems. But that's sort of 70% of my time, I'd say, is just this firefighting of success disasters. And I think the 30% remaining is just much more standard bread and butter growth work where it's more proactive. So, you think about, okay, if we have limited resources, which are the products — we have many different products — which are the products we want to put some juice behind? What does our long-term pricing and packaging look like? Especially given that the technology is changing a lot and behavior and engagement trends are shifting. And then, things like we have a lot of new products coming up. Like, okay, you shipped co-work. Now what? When is the right time that we should lean in as a growth team to start optimizing the core adoption funnel for co-work? So, it's very 70% just crazy firefighting, 30% more bread and butter stuff.
turns.zh: 规模扩张到这种程度会带来很多挑战。如果你想想获客、激活、变现这几个环节，就会发现大量救火式的工作，从一个紧急事务跳到另一个，通常非常痛苦。有趣的是，你看着所有图表，全是绿色，一路向上向右，但每个人还是觉得……情绪上依然很艰难。所以你需要退一步，意识到我们很幸运能有这些问题。但大概 70% 的时间都花在这种成功灾难的救火上。剩下的 30% 是更常规的增长工作，更偏主动。所以你会思考：如果资源有限，我们有很多不同的产品，哪些产品值得投入？长期定价和包装策略是什么？尤其是技术变化很快，用户行为和参与趋势也在变。然后还有很多新产品要上线。比如，你推出了 Co-work，然后呢？增长团队什么时候该介入，开始优化 Co-work 的核心采用漏斗？所以大概 70% 是疯狂的救火，30% 是更常规的工作。

turns.en: Okay, I'm going to dig into a lot of that stuff. One of the cleverest growth moves you all made recently was this idea of importing memory from ChatGPT, where you just made it really easy and kind of jumped on this trend of people getting really excited about Anthropic. Is there anything you could share about the behind-the-scenes story of that feature?
turns.zh: 好的，我会深入探讨这些。你们最近最巧妙的增长举措之一是从 ChatGPT 导入记忆的功能，让用户很容易上手，并抓住了人们对 Anthropic 兴奋的趋势。你能分享一些这个功能背后的故事吗？

turns.en: We were always thinking about what can we do to improve the cold start problem and improve the new user experience. I think that activation is a really big challenge in AI. And so, that's one example of something that we shipped that was very specific to a moment in time. But ultimately, when you zoom out, it's like, okay, how do you really make it easier for people who are signing up to have Claude understand who they are and understand how Claude can help them and get them to the right place.
turns.zh: 我们一直在思考如何改善冷启动问题和新用户体验。我认为激活是 AI 领域一个很大的挑战。所以那是一个例子，我们在特定时间点推出的功能。但归根结底，退一步看，关键是如何让注册用户更容易让 Claude 了解他们是谁，理解 Claude 能如何帮助他们，并引导他们到正确的地方。

turns.en: I want to follow that thread, activation. There's something that comes up a ton when I talk to people leading driving growth on AI products. It's just like there's so much stuff trying to get your attention these days for people to get to a place where they okay, wow, this is really going to be something I want to keep using. It proves to be really hard. And it's also just unreliable. Sometimes it's not going to be magical. It's AI. It's non-deterministic. I guess one is just like how important is focusing on activation, getting people to that aha moment with AI products. And two, what are some things you've learned about how to do that well with Claude or AI in general?
turns.zh: 我想顺着激活这个话题聊下去。我和很多推动 AI 产品增长的人交流时，经常提到一点：现在有太多东西在争夺人们的注意力，要让用户达到“哇，这真是我想一直用的东西”的状态，非常困难。而且也不可靠，有时并不神奇。AI 是非确定性的。所以我想问：第一，专注于激活、让用户达到“顿悟时刻”有多重要？第二，关于如何用 Claude 或 AI 产品做好这一点，你学到了什么？

turns.en: Yeah, it's a good question. I think that activation is critical, right? And defining that is like early activation, call it day zero, day one product experience. I think that historically, anyone who's been in growth or in product understands that this is usually one of the highest levers that you have to actually increase even longer-term retention. And I think that the importance of that has just gotten exponentially higher. Now, zooming out, I feel like one of the biggest problems in the industry is capability overhead, where the models are just getting better so quickly. And the real challenge is on the product side of how do we start to diffuse those benefits to people where even internally, like there's new models coming internally and you're sitting there, you're so busy, and when a new model's available, you need to really carve out time to be like, 'What can this do? How do I need to update my priors?' And if you think about more broadly, for most people, you may have a model that is like AGI or some model that can do all sorts of crazy things, but if people's instinct is to come there and be like, 'Hey, what's the weather in SF?' then, you know, they're not going to get the most out of the product. And so I think that it's tough because the model capabilities are rising so much. So like if I think about, okay, back when we had, I don't know, say like Opus 4, at that point there's a series of things the model can do, and Opus 4.5 unlocked a whole bunch of new things. You can think about, okay, we sit there, we've got this new model Opus 4. The time to then go and run a bunch of tests, figure out, okay, there's the capabilities from this new model. What are the right on-ramps to guide people to those features? You run tests, you get the learnings, you then ship a new flow. By then, you may already have the next model which unlocks newer capabilities that makes all these learnings irrelevant, right? So it's actually just a really difficult problem to stay on top of. I think that many of the same things, same old trends in growth and activation remain accurate where to me it's like ultimately, some of the highest leverages come from finding the right product or the right feature for the right user. And I think that one learning I've seen time and time again across companies actually is that the right friction helps, and adding more friction usually works if you do it the right way. I think that's something I've consistently seen, that we've seen here as well. So to me, I think it's really understanding being able to identify what are the characteristics of the user that allows you to then recommend them to the right feature or product, and not being shy about adding friction to do that. I think is probably the single biggest thing that's important here.
turns.zh: 好问题。我认为激活至关重要。定义早期激活，比如第零天、第一天的产品体验。历史上，任何做过增长或产品的人都知道，这通常是提高长期留存率的最大杠杆之一。而且我认为它的重要性呈指数级增长。退一步看，我觉得行业最大的问题之一是能力开销，模型进步太快了。真正的挑战在产品端，如何将这些好处传递给用户。即使内部，新模型来了，你忙得不可开交，还得专门花时间想：“这个模型能做什么？我需要如何更新我的认知？”更广泛地说，对大多数人而言，你可能有一个 AGI 或能做各种疯狂事情的模型，但如果人们本能地来问“旧金山天气怎么样？”，那他们就不会充分利用产品。所以这很难，因为模型能力提升太快。比如，回想我们之前有 Opus 4，那时模型能做一系列事情，而 Opus 4.5 解锁了一大堆新功能。我们有了新模型 Opus 4，然后花时间跑测试，搞清楚新模型的能力，找到合适的入口引导用户使用这些功能。你跑测试，获得经验，然后推出新流程。但到那时，可能下一个模型已经来了，解锁了更新的能力，让之前的经验都过时了。所以这确实是个很难跟上的问题。我认为增长和激活中很多老规律仍然有效，最终，最高杠杆之一是找到合适的产品或功能给合适的用户。我在多个公司反复看到的一个经验是，适当的摩擦是有帮助的，如果方法得当，增加摩擦通常有效。我在这里也看到了这一点。所以我认为，关键是能够识别用户特征，从而推荐合适的功能或产品，并且不要害怕为此增加摩擦。这可能是最重要的一点。

turns.en: When I asked Ben Mann, one of the co-founders, former podcast guest, what to ask you about, and this is what he highlighted is your experience especially at Mercury redoing onboarding and making it magical. And basically he's in the same place as you have just how important it is for people to understand what the AI tool is capable of to help people decide to use it and stick with it. Is there an example of something you changed in onboarding that helped significantly improve activation?
turns.zh: 我问联合创始人 Ben Mann（之前的播客嘉宾）该问你什么，他特别提到你在 Mercury 重新设计 onboarding 并让它变得神奇的经历。他和你观点一致，认为让人们理解 AI 工具的能力，帮助他们决定使用并坚持使用，非常重要。有没有一个你在 onboarding 中做出的改变，显著提高了激活率的例子？

turns.en: Yeah, it's a great question and I like that he brings up Mercury. I talk about their product a lot. So, worked on the growth team at Mercury. I think it's a fantastic product.
turns.zh: 好问题，我很高兴他提到 Mercury。我经常谈论他们的产品。我在 Mercury 的增长团队工作过，我认为那是一个很棒的产品。

transcript.secZh: 规模化与时间分配挑战

transcript.sec: Mercury's core value: better banking experience

turns.en: It's something many people use and the reason they use it is because the better banking experience than you
turns.zh: 这是很多人使用的东西，他们使用它的原因是它提供了更好的银行体验。

turns.en: Yeah, I'm a very happy customer just to put that out there. I love it. Great product. Highly recommend it. They have personal banking. Everyone's got to go and use it. Right, they just launched that. I think that the interesting thing about Mercury is like the core value is that it's a better experience, right? That's the reason you use it. You're not getting like better other things. It's just a better product experience. And so that ethos is very deeply held within the company. It comes from a number of the founders.
turns.zh: 是的，我是一个非常满意的客户，先说明这一点。我喜欢它。很棒的产品。强烈推荐。他们有个人银行业务。每个人都应该去用。对，他们刚刚推出了这个。我认为 Mercury 有趣的地方在于，其核心价值是更好的体验，对吧？这就是你使用它的原因。你并没有得到其他更好的东西，只是产品体验更好。所以这种理念在公司内部根深蒂固，它来自几位创始人。

transcript.secZh: Mercury 核心价值：卓越银行体验

transcript.sec: Quality focus in onboarding flow at Mercury

turns.en: I think that we had a big push one quarter when I was there on the onboarding flow. So, onboarding flows for banking institutions and regulated entities are extremely complex. Like, the amount of time I spent on the difference between a registered agent address and a legal address and a physical address. These things are very complex and we basically looked at the onboarding flow. We're like, 'Okay, we invested so much in quality in the rest of the product, but we haven't really done it here. And this is the first experience that people have.' And so, we said, 'Forget metrics, forget growth, forget everything else.' As the growth team on conversion, we're going to spend a whole quarter fixing quality in this flow. And so, that's what we did. Like, forget the metrics, we're just going to make this as good of an experience as we can and fix all these like you go back from one field to the other field and adding in your beneficial ownership details. And it actually ended up being like probably until I joined here, the single most impactful quarter that I've ever had as a growth team in terms of the impact that it had. And so, we saw a significant uplift from basically our onboarding started to completion from just focusing on quality. And so, that to me is like a broader learning around quality drives growth that I think I've tried to bring to Anthropic.
turns.zh: 我认为我在那里的时候，有一个季度我们在注册流程上大力投入。因为银行机构和受监管实体的注册流程极其复杂。比如，我花了很多时间区分注册代理地址、法定地址和实际地址之间的差异。这些事情非常复杂，我们基本上审视了注册流程。我们想，‘好吧，我们在产品的其他部分投入了大量精力提升质量，但在这里却没有。而这是人们的第一体验。’于是我们说，‘忘记指标，忘记增长，忘记其他一切。’作为增长团队，负责转化率，我们要花整整一个季度来修复这个流程的质量。于是我们就这么做了。忘记指标，我们只是尽可能让体验变得更好，修复所有问题，比如从一个字段跳转到另一个字段，添加受益所有人详细信息。结果这实际上成了我加入这里之前，作为增长团队所经历的最有影响力的一个季度。我们从注册开始到完成的转化率显著提升，仅仅因为专注于质量。所以对我来说，这是一个更广泛的教训：质量驱动增长，我试图将这一点带到 Anthropic。

transcript.secZh: Mercury 注册流程质量优先

transcript.sec: Anthropic's onboarding: friction as a feature

turns.en: I think for us at Anthropic, some of the things that we've done in the onboarding flow is basically like we ask users questions around who they are, what their interest areas are. And we then use that to recommend different products and features. And a number of people look at the flow and they're like, 'You have so much friction. It's such a long flow.' And I'm like, 'We have the data. We're kind of happy with how that's performing.'
turns.zh: 我认为在 Anthropic，我们在注册流程中做的一些事情基本上是询问用户他们是谁、他们的兴趣领域是什么。然后我们利用这些信息推荐不同的产品和功能。很多人看到这个流程会说，‘你们的摩擦太多了。流程太长了。’而我的回答是，‘我们有数据。我们对它的表现挺满意的。’

transcript.sec: Philosophy on good friction vs bad friction

turns.en: What is your kind of a philosophy on friction, good friction versus bad friction?
turns.zh: 你对摩擦有什么看法，好的摩擦与坏的摩擦？

turns.en: I've just seen time and time again at every job I've been in in growth that adding friction and adding the right steps leads to higher conversion and higher funnel completion. So, you want to get rid of annoying friction that doesn't add value, but the most simple understanding people have is like just solve time to value. Cut all the steps and just get them into the product. And that doesn't work most times. I think if you've really thoroughly tested your flows, I look at the companies I've been at Masterclass. If you go through Masterclass's purchase flow right now, you'll go through all these steps in this quiz when you land on when you're trying to buy and it's like I came here to buy and it's taking me through all these questions. What are you here for etc. I think it's easy to look at that and be like why do they have this? This is a terrible thing. Just cut it all. And it's like no, that's been thoroughly tested and actually that was a significant revenue driver because it helps users feel that the product is for them by understanding what their interests are and then recommending the content and classes there. One of the growth PMs on our team left to join Calm, the meditation app. If you go to Calm's landing page and go through their purchase flow, their login flow, you'll see a quiz. It's not a coincidence. At Mercury we also tested I think you might post on Twitter like we broke out some steps in onboarding and just having one screen, if you have five or six different form inputs and you often break that into two screens and reduce the cognitive load, that is something that performs well. We added steps into the flow there that actually performed well. Same with at Anthropic. So, I think the takeaway to me is like cut friction when it doesn't add to the experience of helping a user understand why the product is for them. But, if you can help users understand why a product is for them and how to use it and what's most relevant to them and it's going to add friction, don't shy away from it. Test it, confirm that it works for you, but I think this is something that most growth practitioners deeply understand.
turns.zh: 我在每一份增长工作中都一次又一次地看到，增加摩擦、增加正确的步骤会带来更高的转化率和更高的漏斗完成率。所以，你想要消除那些不增加价值的烦人摩擦，但人们最简单的理解是，只要缩短价值实现时间就行了。砍掉所有步骤，直接让他们进入产品。但大多数时候这行不通。我认为如果你真的彻底测试过你的流程，看看我待过的公司 Masterclass。如果你现在走一遍 Masterclass 的购买流程，当你登陆并试图购买时，你会经历一个测验的所有步骤，你会想‘我是来买的，却要我回答这么多问题。你来这里是为了什么等等。’很容易认为‘他们为什么这么做？这太糟糕了。全部砍掉。’但事实并非如此，这经过了彻底测试，实际上它是一个重要的收入驱动因素，因为它通过了解用户的兴趣，然后推荐内容和课程，帮助用户感觉产品是为他们量身定做的。我们团队的一位增长产品经理离职去了 Calm，那个冥想应用。如果你去 Calm 的着陆页，走一遍他们的购买流程、登录流程，你会看到一个测验。这不是巧合。在 Mercury 我们也测试过，我想你可能在 Twitter 上发过，我们把注册流程中的一些步骤拆分出来，比如如果你有五六种不同的表单输入，你经常把它拆成两个屏幕，减少认知负荷，这表现很好。我们在流程中增加了一些步骤，实际上表现也很好。在 Anthropic 也一样。所以，我认为给我的启示是：当摩擦不能帮助用户理解产品为什么适合他们时，就消除它。但是，如果你能帮助用户理解产品为什么适合他们、如何使用以及什么对他们最相关，而这会增加摩擦，不要回避它。测试它，确认它对你有用，但我认为这是大多数增长从业者都深刻理解的东西。

transcript.sec: Understanding users for lifecycle and targeting

turns.en: And the 'for them' is really important there, which you described as adding friction to better understand who they are so that you know how to recommend the right thing for them.
turns.zh: 而“为他们”这一点非常重要，你描述为增加摩擦以更好地了解他们是谁，从而知道如何为他们推荐合适的东西。

turns.en: Correct. Yes. And done right, that just flows through, right? So, it helps you with activation, but then it helps you with life cycle. You know more about those users and why they are here and in our most sophisticated businesses, you can then even if someone drops off, you can do look-alike targeting and you can get them at the ad layer as well. So, that initial piece of how you understand who the user is is just like a juice that keeps on giving if you then use it right further down funnel.
turns.zh: 没错。是的。如果做得好，这就会贯穿始终，对吧？所以它有助于激活，然后有助于生命周期。你对这些用户了解得更多，知道他们为什么在这里，在我们最成熟的业务中，即使有人流失，你也可以做相似人群定向，在广告层也能触达他们。所以，最初了解用户是谁这一部分就像是一笔持续回报的投资，如果你在漏斗下游正确使用它的话。

transcript.secZh: 理解用户生命周期与定位

transcript.sec: AI for competitive analysis and tear-downs

turns.en: Everyone's about to go do a bunch of tear-downs of Cloud's onboarding, MasterClass onboarding, Mercury onboarding. Kind of as a tangent, I was at this PM dinner recently and I was asking all the PMs how has your role as a PM changed most with AI? Like where is AI most impacting what you do? And one of the PMs answered that is actually doing competitive analysis, doing a bunch of tear-downs of what other people are doing for pricing pages and onboarding. So, it's easy to do now. Just hey, co-work. I don't know, would you co-work for this or Cloud? What would you use for that? Okay, this is good. Help people pick which tool to use.
turns.zh: 大家都准备对 Cloud 的注册流程、MasterClass 的注册流程、Mercury 的注册流程做一大堆拆解分析。顺便说一句，我最近参加了一个产品经理晚宴，我问所有产品经理，你的角色在 AI 影响下变化最大的是什么？AI 对你工作的影响最大在哪里？其中一位产品经理回答说，实际上是做竞争分析，对别人的定价页面和注册流程做大量拆解。所以现在做这些很容易。只要说‘嘿，co-work。’我不知道，你会用 co-work 还是 Cloud 来做这个？你会用什么？好吧，这个不错。帮助人们选择使用哪个工具。

transcript.sec: Growth team structure and focus

turns.en: If you want to go do a bunch of tear-downs of other competitors onboarding flows, what would you use? You can use Coda for this, right? So, if you have Coda with the Chrome extension, task it to go and look for these flows and show me a view of what's working, what's not. That's definitely something that Coda can do. Cool. I think that's one of the challenges: you have all these tools now and just which one's for me. By the way, the Chrome extension, I use it all the time. It's amazing. I want to drill a little bit further into the growth org. There's this whole meme on Twitter the other day of just like you have one growth marketer driving all growth at Anthropic and it's like, 'Okay, that's crazy.' What's like how many growth people are there? What's kind of the rough org structure of the growth team?
turns.zh: 如果你想对竞争对手的注册流程做大量拆解，你会用什么工具？你可以用 Coda 来做，对吧？所以，如果你有带 Chrome 扩展的 Coda，让它去查找这些流程，然后给我展示哪些有效、哪些无效。这绝对是 Coda 能做的事情。酷。我觉得其中一个挑战是：你现在有这么多工具，不知道哪个适合自己。顺便说一句，那个 Chrome 扩展我一直在用，太棒了。我想进一步了解一下增长团队。前几天 Twitter 上有个梗，说 Anthropic 只有一个增长营销人员在推动所有增长，这听起来有点疯狂。你们有多少增长人员？增长团队的大致组织结构是怎样的？

turns.en: We're roughly maybe 40 people now. I think we are structured very much like a traditional growth team in that we have horizontals of growth platform and monetization who think about the sphere of growth across the entirety of our products, and then we have more audience-focused growth pods. You can think about B2B growth, Claude Code growth, knowledge worker growth, API growth. So really these audiences to keep a narrow focus, which is a thing you have to do when you have so many different products. And then these horizontals that think about things across the board.
turns.zh: 我们现在大概有 40 人左右。我认为我们的结构非常像传统的增长团队，我们有增长平台和变现的横向团队，负责考虑我们所有产品中的增长领域，然后我们有更多面向受众的增长小组。你可以想象 B2B 增长、Claude Code 增长、知识工作者增长、API 增长。所以这些受众需要保持狭窄的焦点，这是当你拥有这么多不同产品时必须做的事情。然后这些横向团队负责全面考虑问题。

turns.en: And is it a cross team of engineers, designers, PMs? What's kind of the functions within the growth org?
turns.zh: 这是一个由工程师、设计师、产品经理组成的跨职能团队吗？增长组织内部有哪些职能？

turns.en: Yeah, it's engineers, designers, PMs, data. I think that overall the shape of the org is quite similar to a traditional growth team. Probably the things that are maybe different is that we index a lot more towards larger swings as opposed to smaller optimizations. Like if I think about a traditional growth team, I would have probably done maybe 60-70% of my time on small to medium bets, 20-30% on larger swings, and I think that for us we flip it a lot. We do much more the other way, where it's sort of 70/30 or more like 50/50, rather than indexing towards smaller bets. That's probably one of the biggest changes, I think.
turns.zh: 是的，包括工程师、设计师、产品经理、数据人员。我认为整体组织形态与传统的增长团队非常相似。可能不同的地方在于，我们更倾向于大的赌注，而不是小的优化。比如，如果我想一个传统的增长团队，我可能会把 60-70% 的时间花在中小型赌注上，20-30% 花在大的赌注上，而对我们来说，我们翻转了很多。我们更多地走另一条路，大概是 70/30 或者更接近 50/50，而不是偏向小赌注。这可能是最大的变化之一。

turns.en: Just to highlight that, what's interesting there is at the scale you guys are at, a 1% win is massive in the scheme of things. So, it's interesting that even at the scale you're at, you're not focusing on these micro-optimizations. It is easy, you could easily focus on these small optimizations, and then you tally them up at the end of the quarter, and you're like, 'Look how much impact we made.' And you could do that.
turns.zh: 需要强调的是，有趣的是，在你们所处的规模上，1% 的胜利在整体上也是巨大的。所以，有趣的是，即使你们处于这个规模，你们也没有专注于这些微优化。这很容易，你可以轻松地专注于这些小优化，然后在季度末把它们加起来，然后说，‘看我们产生了多大的影响。’你可以这样做。

turns.en: Another billion. No big deal.
turns.zh: 再来十亿。没什么大不了的。

turns.en: Yeah, but the thing is we've been tracking at 10x year-on-year, and that's the thing we keep in mind. I think it ultimately comes down to our fixation in this company about the exponential. I think if you look at anyone talking from Anthropic about basically anything, we always talk about the exponential. It's effectively as model capabilities continue to grow on an exponential, and the tools around them enable a better job of diffusing that into useful use cases, you basically just keep unlocking new markets where the value of those markets significantly dwarfs what the value of the previous markets were. Agentic coding is a great example. It didn't exist a year and a half ago. And now just the value of agentic coding is bigger than the previous market of AI coding use. And I think that is the core thing here, which is that the future product value is an order of magnitude higher than it is today. I think about a normal business, like a trading app or a grocery delivery product. Many of these leading companies are great businesses, but if I think about what is the product value that a company like a standard grocery delivery app gives you as an end user today versus two years from now? I look at it as again two years from now, even if you're shipping all these new products, as an end user the value you get from that product maybe goes up 30 to 50% if the company's done a really good job of shipping new features. It's not exponential though. And so if I think about okay, you're here today, in two years you're going to have 30 to 50% more product value. Then as a growth team, that relative differential of the product value two years from now relative to today, I can actually capture a decent percentage of that with the small to medium optimizations that typically have higher conviction as opposed to larger bets. But for Anthropic it's not really that way because of the exponential and our products being very much the product value coming from AI, the product value that we will deliver in two years time is probably a hundred to a thousand times what it is today. And so if I think about that, there's so much value on offer. You need to shift more towards okay, we need to take larger bets and then we need to not miss the forest for the trees. That's why we still do all the small optimizations. It really matters and no one else is going to do some of these things, so we need to do it. The compounding value is not immaterial, but we do take on much larger core product type of swings as well.
turns.zh: 是的，但问题是，我们一直保持着 10 倍的同比增长，这是我们牢记在心的。我认为这最终归结为我们公司对指数增长的执着。我认为如果你看 Anthropic 的人谈论任何事情，我们总是谈论指数增长。实际上，随着模型能力继续指数级增长，围绕它们的工具能够更好地将其扩散到有用的用例中，你基本上不断解锁新市场，这些市场的价值远远超过以前市场的价值。智能体式编码就是一个很好的例子。一年半前它还不存在。而现在，智能体式编码的价值已经超过了之前 AI 编码使用的市场。我认为这里的核心是，未来的产品价值比今天高一个数量级。我想一个普通的企业，比如一个交易应用或一个杂货配送产品。许多这些领先的公司都是伟大的企业，但如果我想一个标准的杂货配送应用，作为最终用户，你今天获得的产品价值与两年后相比如何？我认为两年后，即使你发布了所有这些新产品，作为最终用户，你从该产品中获得的价值可能只增加 30% 到 50%，如果公司在发布新功能方面做得非常好的话。但这并不是指数级的。所以，如果我想，好吧，你今天在这里，两年后你将拥有 30% 到 50% 更多的产品价值。那么作为一个增长团队，相对于今天，两年后产品价值的相对差异，我实际上可以通过中小型优化（通常具有更高的确定性）来捕获其中的相当一部分，而不是大的赌注。但对于 Anthropic 来说，情况并非如此，因为指数增长和我们的产品价值来自 AI，我们在两年内交付的产品价值可能是今天的 100 到 1000 倍。所以，如果我想这个，有太多的价值可供选择。你需要更多地转向，好吧，我们需要下更大的赌注，然后我们不能只见树木不见森林。这就是为什么我们仍然做所有的小优化。这真的很重要，而且没有其他人会做这些事情，所以我们需要做。复合价值并非微不足道，但我们也承担了更大的核心产品类型的赌注。

turns.en: So you mentioned that the Chrome extension is now the thing that underpins a number of use cases on Coda and Claude Code as well. And that's something that the growth team built. That's a very AI-filled product that is a very research-heavy product, but we were just bullish on it. We had an engineer who was very bullish on it, and we were just like, 'Hey, no one else is doing it. We're going to do it.' And so, that's the sort of thing that I wouldn't have done at another company. Oh, wow. That's I did not know that.
turns.zh: 所以你提到 Chrome 扩展现在是支撑 Coda 和 Claude Code 上许多用例的东西。那是增长团队构建的。那是一个充满 AI 的产品，一个研究密集型的产品，但我们就是看好它。我们有一个工程师非常看好它，我们就说，‘嘿，没有其他人做这个。我们要做。’所以，这种事情我在其他公司是不会做的。哦，哇。这我还真不知道。

transcript.sec: Advice for AI-first companies

turns.en: So, the takeaway here is that there's stuff to extract from your advice that is only true at Anthropic, and then there's what other companies can learn from this experience. One is your sense that if you're working in AI, shift more of the pie chart towards bigger bets because in the future, the opportunity is so large, you want to find those as soon as possible versus micro optimize?
turns.zh: 那么，这里的一个要点是，你的建议中有些东西只在 Anthropic 成立，而其他公司可以从你的经验中学到什么。一是你的感觉是，如果你在 AI 领域工作，应该把更多的资源转向更大的赌注，因为未来的机会如此之大，你想尽快找到它们，而不是微观优化？

turns.en: To be more specific, I'd say that if the primary value your product delivers is underpinned by AI as a central element, then you should operate this way. Companies like Lovable, Cursor, all these great businesses, as the exponential rises, their value props are also going to continue to rise significantly. If you're building an AI-first product, I would definitely operate this way. If you're building a product where AI is not the core, and you have some AI features on the side, I don't know that I would operate this way. It would depend on how the rest of the product is staffed and how growth is staffed in relation to that.
turns.zh: 更具体地说，如果你的产品的主要价值是由 AI 作为核心元素支撑的，那么你应该这样运作。像 Lovable、Cursor 这样的公司，随着指数级增长，它们的价值主张也会显著提升。如果你在构建一个 AI 优先的产品，我肯定会这样运作。如果你构建的产品并非 AI 优先，而只是附带一些 AI 功能，我不确定我会这样运作。这取决于产品的其他部分如何配备人员，以及增长团队如何与之相关。

transcript.sec: Growth team structure for multiple products

turns.en: And then in terms of the way you are structured, I thought that was really interesting. It's a combination of different sorts of things. There's the API growth, Claude Code growth, but then also personas, like a vertical of knowledge workers and B2B. Is that intentional? Like some specific bets and one just kind of broad market opportunity?
turns.zh: 然后关于你们的组织结构，我觉得很有意思。这是不同事物的组合。有 API 增长、Claude Code 增长，还有用户画像，比如知识工作者和 B2B 的垂直领域。这是有意为之吗？比如一些具体的赌注和一个广泛的市场机会？

turns.en: When you have one product, it's easier to have a growth team that's more purely on the funnel, right? You have conversion, activation, monetization. But as you start to have multiple products, I think that's harder because if you have one activation team for example, but then you have Code, Co-work, and all the other things, they're very different audiences and very different sets of cross-functional stakeholders internally. So we're looking at what structure allows us to have as much focus. Focus is a really big thing on audience and problems, and also the ties to cross-functional partners is really important. Our folks in the Claude Code growth team work extremely closely with Cat and Boris and the others on that team. So that tie-in is really important as well.
turns.zh: 当你只有一个产品时，更容易有一个纯粹专注于漏斗的增长团队，对吧？你有转化、激活、变现。但当你开始有多个产品时，我认为这更难了，因为如果你只有一个激活团队，但你有 Code、Co-work 和其他东西，它们的受众非常不同，内部的跨职能利益相关者也不同。所以我们正在寻找一种能让我们保持专注的结构。专注在受众和问题上非常重要，与跨职能合作伙伴的联系也非常重要。我们 Claude Code 增长团队的人与 Cat、Boris 以及该团队的其他成员合作非常紧密。所以这种联系也非常重要。

transcript.sec: How growth is changing with AI

turns.en: You've done growth at a lot of different companies, basically traditional growth before Anthropic. How else is growth as a function and as a skill set changing with the rise of AI products and AI startups?
turns.zh: 你在很多不同的公司做过增长，基本上是在 Anthropic 之前的传统增长。随着 AI 产品和 AI 初创公司的兴起，增长作为一个职能和技能组合还在如何变化？

turns.en: I think if your core product value is very backed by AI, then it is shifting where you skew more towards larger bets as opposed to smaller medium experiments. Another big thing related to this that I think will accelerate is we are starting to look at how to automate growth. Our growth platform team, we have Alexey Komissarouk who teaches growth engineering at Reforge, and he is driving an effort called CASH — Claude Accelerates Sustainable Hacker Growth. I didn't come up with that. But it's an effort to look at how we can use Claude to automate growth experimentation. It is still very small and very early. We kicked it off only a couple of months ago. Before Opus 4.5 it wasn't really possible, but more recently with Opus 4.6 we're seeing it head in the right direction. I think this will happen more and more across the industry. If you think about the lifecycle of shipping, there are four parts: identifying opportunities, building the feature, testing and ensuring quality, and then analyzing data. We think about it in those four ways and score how good Claude is in each area. We've been testing this at small scale with copy changes and minor UI tweaks. It delivers results. You can press play and it prints money. The win rate is like what I'd expect from a junior PM with 2-3 years of experience, not quite at senior PM level. But it wasn't available at all a couple of months ago, so it's getting better rapidly. I think it will change where you can do this for larger experiments. But the one piece I didn't mention is cross-functional stakeholder management. There's still a need for human brains.
turns.zh: 我认为如果你的核心产品价值非常依赖 AI，那么它正在转向更大的赌注，而不是中小型实验。与此相关的另一件大事是，我们开始研究如何自动化增长。我们的增长平台团队有 Alexey Komissarouk，他在 Reforge 教授增长工程，他正在推动一个名为 CASH（Claude 加速可持续黑客增长）的项目。这不是我起的名字。但这是一项努力，研究如何使用 Claude 来自动化增长实验。它仍然非常小，非常早期。我们几个月前才启动。在 Opus 4.5 之前，这几乎不可能，但最近随着 Opus 4.6，我们看到它朝着正确的方向发展。我认为这将在整个行业越来越普遍。如果你考虑交付的生命周期，有四个部分：识别机会、构建功能、测试和确保质量，然后分析数据。我们从这四个方面思考，并评估 Claude 在每个方面的表现。我们一直在小规模测试，涉及文案更改和微小的 UI 调整。它确实产生了结果。你可以按下播放键，它就能赚钱。胜率大约相当于一个 2-3 年经验的初级 PM，还不到高级 PM 的水平。但几个月前这根本不可能，所以它正在快速进步。我认为它将改变你进行更大规模实验的方式。但我没有提到的一个部分是跨职能利益相关者管理。仍然需要人类的大脑。

transcript.sec: PM work not going away

turns.en: And I think that one is going to mean that in my eyes, the work of PMs is not going away anytime soon. That piece, especially for larger projects, you don't need to do as much of it for smaller stuff, right? You can skip it, but for larger stuff, that piece is not going away until the other stakeholders are their own little agents running around.
turns.zh: 我认为这意味着，在我看来，产品经理的工作短期内不会消失。对于较大的项目，你不需要像小项目那样做那么多，对吧？你可以跳过，但对于较大的项目，这部分工作不会消失，直到其他利益相关者都变成他们自己的小智能体在四处运作。

turns.en: That's right. I think that would be the point where that changes. It's funny. We had a difficult meeting a couple of weeks ago, and me and my head of design, Joel, we were debriefing afterwards. He thinks, and he's just like, "Oh, we will have AGI and it will still be impossible to get six people in a room to align." And I'm like, "Yeah, I think I can see that." That's like, what's the harder alignment problem?
turns.zh: 没错。我认为那才是改变的时候。有趣的是，几周前我们开了一个很困难的会，之后我和我的设计主管 Joel 进行了复盘。他认为，而且他直接说：“哦，就算有了 AGI，让六个人在一个房间里达成一致仍然是不可能的。”我回答说：“是啊，我觉得我能理解。”这就像是，哪个对齐问题更难？

transcript.sec: AI-driven growth experiments

turns.en: Okay, this is so interesting and this is exactly where I feel like things are going. So just to be clear what you shared here, there's basically this tool that comes up with experiments to run to help grow Claude and all the tools. So it comes up with an idea, somebody looks at them, approves, cool, let's do these things, builds it, ships it, tests the results, sees how it's doing, and then it comes back with like, here's things that are working. Is that roughly right?
turns.zh: 好的，这太有趣了，这正是我感觉事情发展的方向。所以澄清一下你分享的内容，基本上有一个工具会提出要运行的实验，帮助增长 Claude 和所有工具。它提出想法，有人查看并批准，好，我们来做这些事，构建、发布、测试结果，看看效果如何，然后它反馈说，这些是有效的东西。大致是这样吗？

turns.en: It's roughly right. Right now we have a human in the loop approving, but the amount of time... I can't think about scaling this way of just week on week: are things getting better in each of the areas? Are people spending less time on each of the areas? Are your results getting better in each of the areas? And as long as week on week that's getting better, then you're like, okay, this whole initiative is scaling. So that's roughly right, but you can think about it as a lot of this can be automated where human review is not needed. We care a lot about brand, right? So that is something we do look at right now. We don't want to be shipping something that goes against the brand. But then you can have a skill that contains your brand guidelines and very clear do's and don'ts on brand. All of these types of accompaniments, I think, are going to get better. The models then get better at understanding how to use them, so over time, I think the need for human review decreases significantly.
turns.zh: 大致正确。目前我们有一个人工审批环节，但我无法想象以这种方式进行 Scaling——每周看各个领域是否在进步？人们花在每个领域的时间是否减少？每个领域的结果是否变好？只要每周都在变好，你就会觉得，好吧，整个计划正在 Scaling。所以大致正确，但你可以认为其中很多可以自动化，不需要人工审核。我们非常在意品牌，对吧？所以目前我们确实会检查这一点。我们不想发布违背品牌的东西。但你可以有一个技能，包含你的品牌指南和非常明确的品牌注意事项。所有这些配套的东西，我认为会越来越好。模型也会越来越擅长使用它们，所以随着时间的推移，我认为人工审核的需求会显著减少。

turns.en: Yeah, and you could always unship it if it's like, oh yeah, that was actually not a great idea. And that's such a good point that we think we need people to do these things forever, and it turns out, okay, a skill could do this really well. Here's our brand guidelines, here's our vision, here's our mission, here's our goals, here's what matters to us. Okay, let's not ship that thing.
turns.zh: 是的，而且你随时可以撤回，如果觉得，哦，那其实不是个好主意。这一点说得很好，我们以为永远需要人来做这些事，结果发现，一个技能就能做得很好。这是我们的品牌指南，这是我们的愿景，这是我们的使命，这是我们的目标，这是我们在乎的东西。好了，别发布那个东西。

transcript.sec: AI expanding in product development

turns.en: So the reason I think this is so interesting, this is like I've just been watching the expansion of AI doing more of the product development process, in the growth process, of just, okay, it from helping you write code to like writing all your code to reviewing your code. Now it feels like what are the other ends of this, the two ends around this? It's kind of going from the middle out. The top of that is coming up with what to do, and then there's like the alignment stuff still very hard. And then on the other end it's reviewing the code and then shipping it and then getting distribution, that's like a whole other thing I want to talk to you about. So what I'm hearing here and this is exactly what I thought was going to start happening is AI is now getting really good at telling us what to do, not just taking our orders and building it. And it feels like the growth version of this is where it starts because it's so much simpler, not that growth is easy, but just like it's data driven, there's this loop that you talk about. So I think this is such an interesting sign of things to come across just generally product.
turns.zh: 所以我认为这非常有趣，就像我一直在观察 AI 在产品开发过程中承担更多工作，在增长过程中，从帮你写代码，到写所有代码，再到审查代码。现在感觉另外两端是什么？它似乎是从中间向外扩展。顶端是决定做什么，对齐仍然很难。另一端是审查代码、发布和获取分发，这完全是另一回事，我想和你聊聊。所以我在这里听到的，正是我认为将要开始发生的事情：AI 现在非常擅长告诉我们该做什么，而不仅仅是接受指令并构建。感觉增长版本是起点，因为它更简单——不是说增长容易，而是它数据驱动，有你提到的那个循环。所以我认为这是一个非常有趣的信号，预示着产品领域的未来。

turns.en: Yeah, definitely. Just putting that out there.
turns.zh: 是的，确实如此。只是提一下。

transcript.sec: Future of PM, design, engineering roles

turns.en: Okay, so something that I'm constantly thinking about along these lines is just the future of product, PM, engineering, how those roles shift over time based on the stuff we're talking about. How are you working together as a triad? And where do you see these roles going? What's most going to change do you think across these three roles?
turns.zh: 好的，我一直在思考的一个问题是产品、产品经理、工程角色的未来，这些角色如何随着我们讨论的内容而演变。你们作为三人组是如何协作的？你认为这些角色会走向何方？这三个角色中，哪个变化最大？

turns.en: This is something we talk about and think about frequently, and the picture changes rapidly. Sometimes when things break, historically in the past, it's like, okay, now you need to hire more engineers, you need to hire more designers, etc., and it's more just a lifecycle thing of where the lifecycle of your team and that specific pod is to identify where the bottleneck is. But now when things break, you still need to look at it: is it the actual ratio, or is there also underlying technological shifts that are causing this to break? So that's an interesting thing. I think that smaller companies, if you find me a 15 or 20 person company with the only PM there working with some designers and engineers, I think in the smaller companies you'll see probably the biggest blend, where the PM will be doing all sorts of things: designing, shipping, etc. And I think you just have extreme bifurcation. At larger organizations, more scaled organizations, I think the jury is still very much out. You speak to people even internally here, and different people in different teams have different views of how much these roles are coming together versus how much they will be separate. I think that it's not to say that even the PMs, a number of PMs are shipping and pushing PRs, etc. But if I look at what I'm seeing, I think it's clear that while PMs and designers are getting more leverage from AI, engineering is getting the most leverage right now. I look at tools like Claude Code, and the amount of leverage engineers are getting from them is higher than the leverage that designers and PMs are getting from them today. Now this is rapidly changing, but that is my view today. So if you think about the default team, which is say five engineers, one designer, one PM, with Claude Code, that five engineers is like 2-3x'd, right? And the PMs and designers have also increased, but they are now managing what is effectively a much larger group of engineers.
turns.zh: 这是我们经常讨论和思考的事情，情况变化很快。过去当事情出问题时，通常是你需要招聘更多工程师、更多设计师等，这更像是团队和特定小组的生命周期问题，需要找出瓶颈所在。但现在当事情出问题时，你仍然需要审视：是比例问题，还是也有潜在的技术转变导致问题？所以这很有趣。我认为在较小的公司，比如一个 15 或 20 人的公司，只有一个产品经理与一些设计师和工程师合作，你会看到最大的融合，产品经理会做各种事情：设计、发布等。我认为会出现极端分化。在较大的组织、更规模化的组织中，我认为尚无定论。即使在这里内部，不同团队的人对角色融合与分离的程度也有不同看法。我想说的是，甚至很多产品经理也在发布代码、提交 PR 等。但根据我的观察，很明显，虽然产品经理和设计师从 AI 中获得了更多杠杆，但工程目前获得的杠杆最大。我看看像 Claude Code 这样的工具，工程师从中获得的杠杆比设计师和产品经理今天获得的要高。虽然这正在迅速变化，但这是我今天的看法。所以如果你考虑一个默认团队，比如五个工程师、一个设计师、一个产品经理，有了 Claude Code，那五个工程师的效率提高了 2 到 3 倍，对吧？产品经理和设计师的效率也提高了，但他们现在实际上是在管理一个更大的工程师团队。

transcript.sec: PM and design strain due to AI acceleration

turns.en: And so even though the head count in the org structure hasn't changed, you're now dealing with a situation of maybe 15 to 20 engineers in the old world, 1.5 to 2 PMs, and maybe 1.5 to 2 designers. So we're seeing that putting a lot of strain on PM and design. It's not everywhere. I look at teams like Cloud Code and I think that because that product is so technical, it's probably the right thing where the PMs are all basically engineers themselves anyway. But we had a product like a PM lead on site the other week and we were all talking about this where across the board their feeling is PM and design is just squeezed. It's just absolutely squeezed. And we're like, is it the right thing here that we just need to hire a ton more PMs? That could actually be where we land.
turns.zh: 所以尽管组织架构中的人数没有变化，但现在你面对的情况是：过去可能有 15 到 20 个工程师、1.5 到 2 个产品经理、1.5 到 2 个设计师。我们看到这给产品经理和设计带来了很大压力。并非所有团队都这样。我看像 Cloud Code 这样的团队，因为产品非常技术化，产品经理本身基本上都是工程师，所以可能还好。但前几周我们有一个产品负责人来现场，大家都在讨论，普遍感觉产品经理和设计被压得喘不过气来。我们就在想，是不是应该大量招聘产品经理？这可能是最终的解决方案。

turns.en: You know, on growth how I think about it is like one, we are hiring a number of growth PMs. We desperately need people who are very very good and we are hiring. So if you are excited by what we're doing and you know growth, please feel free to apply. I would love to chat. And maybe craft an amazing cold email to you. Yeah, feel free to craft that email. So that's one: I think we are going to be hiring a number of PMs. But the second thing we do is we very much hire product-minded engineers. I think this has always been the best thing to do in growth. You always want to have the engineers coming up with ideas. So we especially want people who can really step in as mini PMs if the PM is absent. So we're basically more formally leveraging that right now because we are so stretched. The frame we have is that if a project is less than two weeks of engineering time or less, then the engineer is on the hook to effectively be the PM for that. That means things like talking to security, legal, and cross-functional stakeholders. The engineer is very much driving that. The PM will get looped in and advise if needed. If something is wildly off track, they'll step in, but they're much more in an advisory capacity versus execution. If a project is more than two engineering weeks, then the default is that the PM should continue to be on the hook for making that go well. They'll delegate more to eng, but they're squarely accountable. It's not fully clean cut. Use your head. If this is a one-week thing but extremely controversial, the PM should probably still drive it. But that's the approach I expect more companies will start to do: deputize the engineers to be mini PMs. Not everyone can do it. So the engineers who are more product-minded, their value goes up significantly, like an order of magnitude. And I think we will probably still be hiring a lot of PMs.
turns.zh: 关于增长，我是这么想的：第一，我们正在招聘一批增长产品经理。我们迫切需要非常优秀的人才，我们正在招人。所以如果你对我们做的事情感到兴奋，并且了解增长，请随时申请。我很乐意聊聊。也许可以给你写一封很棒的冷邮件。是的，尽管写那封邮件。所以这是第一点：我认为我们会招聘很多产品经理。但第二点是，我们非常注重招聘有产品思维的工程师。我认为这在增长领域一直是最好的做法。你总是希望工程师能提出想法。所以我们特别需要那些能够在产品经理缺席时充当迷你产品经理的人。由于我们目前人手非常紧张，我们正在更正式地利用这一点。我们的框架是：如果一个项目需要不到两周的工程时间，那么工程师就要负责有效地充当该项目的产品经理。这意味着要跟安全、法务和跨职能利益相关者沟通。工程师要主导这些。产品经理会被拉进来，在需要时提供建议。如果事情严重偏离轨道，他们会介入，但更多是顾问角色而非执行角色。如果一个项目超过两周工程时间，那么默认产品经理应继续负责确保项目顺利进行。他们会更多地向工程授权，但他们对结果负全责。这并非完全一刀切。要动脑子。如果是一个一周的项目但极具争议，产品经理可能仍应主导。但我认为更多公司会开始采用这种方法：让工程师充当迷你产品经理。不是每个人都能做到。所以那些更有产品思维的工程师，他们的价值会显著提升，比如一个数量级。而且我认为我们可能仍然会招聘很多产品经理。

turns.en: There is so much interesting stuff I want to follow up on here. Okay, so one is this idea of two weeks just briefly. I always joke as a PM, you can go on vacation and be away for a couple weeks from your team, and things are going to be all right. There's momentum, there's a plan, people will keep operating. And it feels like that's kind of a rule of thumb you use: if it's a two-week project, you'll be all right without a PM, you can handle it. I love that those two connect. Okay, the other here is so interesting. So, you're saying here that because engineers are so accelerated and this all makes sense, PMs and design are kind of just like holy sh— there's like hard to keep up with the space of engineering. And what you're saying is you need more and more PMs to keep up. That's one route, or it's engineers that can PM essentially. Which is so funny. It's just like okay, great use for product managers until more of the PM-y stuff can be done by AI. But that's a really interesting trend. I don't know, is there anything else there just like oh wow, we actually may need more PMs. The ratio more PMs, fewer engineers might be the future.
turns.zh: 这里有很多有趣的东西我想跟进。好，第一个是关于两周这个想法。我作为产品经理经常开玩笑说，你可以去度假离开团队几周，一切都会没事的。有 momentum，有计划，人们会继续运作。感觉这有点像你用的经验法则：如果是两周的项目，没有产品经理也没问题，你能搞定。我喜欢这两者的联系。好，另一个点非常有趣。你说因为工程师速度大大加快，这很合理，产品经理和设计就有点跟不上工程的速度了。你说要么需要越来越多的产品经理来跟上，要么就是工程师本质上能当产品经理。这很有趣。就像说，好吧，产品经理的用途很好，直到更多产品经理的工作能被 AI 完成。但这是一个非常有趣的趋势。我不知道，还有别的吗？比如，哇，我们可能真的需要更多产品经理。更多产品经理、更少工程师的比例可能是未来。

turns.en: Yeah, I think this really depends on the industry and the size of company. Any company where you're building something that's much more developer focused, you're going to rely on the engineers a lot more. Earlier companies don't have as much of this cross-functional coordination, stakeholder alignment nonsense that you need all these PMs for. So you can get by with less. But as a company scales, then if I think about okay, now you have this ratio where maybe the one PM became two PMs from productivity, the five engineers became 20 engineers, the one designer maybe became three designers. If you think about what is the best use of time for that PM? I think this is a really interesting thing: how much should PMs be actually shipping things themselves versus everything else. In the world where you're limited on engineering, the PM should definitely be shipping things. In today's world, this is a good way to get an understanding of the tools, which is really important. So the PM should be shipping for that reason. But if I'm one PM or two PMs and there are 20 engineers, I think about what is the incremental value I can add with my time. Is it actually shipping the 21st PM feature, or is it saying how am I getting a little bit better at guiding the team on what the right opportunities are? So that's where I think in this world, you may have all these engineers who are like mini PMs. And the better that happens, that's where I would love to be doing more of. But still, if you then get a really good PM who can come in and improve the why and the what particularly by 5%, that is such a high leverage hire.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为这完全取决于行业和公司规模。任何构建更偏开发者产品的公司，你会更多地依赖工程师。早期公司没有那么多跨职能协调、利益相关者对齐这些需要产品经理的琐事。所以你可以用更少的产品经理。但随着公司规模扩大，假设比例从 1 个产品经理变成 2 个，5 个工程师变成 20 个，1 个设计师变成 3 个。那么产品经理的时间最佳用途是什么？我认为这是一个非常有趣的问题：产品经理应该花多少时间自己交付东西，多少时间做其他事情。在工程资源有限的世界里，产品经理肯定应该自己交付东西。在当今世界，这是了解工具的好方法，非常重要。所以产品经理应该为此而交付。但如果我是一两个产品经理，面对 20 个工程师，我会想我的时间能带来多少增量价值。是交付第 21 个产品经理功能，还是更好地引导团队找到正确的机会？所以我认为在这个世界里，你可能有很多像迷你产品经理一样的工程师。这种情况越好，我就越愿意多做。但即便如此，如果你能招到一个非常优秀的产品经理，他能把“为什么”和“做什么”提升 5%，那将是一个高杠杆的招聘。

turns.en: This is such an interesting insight you're making. It's so counter to how a lot of people are thinking PM is evolving. What I'm hearing here is because PMs are so behind, because engineers are just getting so much done, there's like many people here, okay, you need to be prototyping, you need to be shipping PRs as a PM. What you're saying, which I completely agree with, is your time is much better spent helping PM basically, and helping the engineers become better PMs themselves. And the leverage there is a lot higher than you spending time coding shipping PRs in most cases.
turns.zh: 你提出的这个见解非常有趣。这与很多人对产品经理角色演变的看法截然相反。我听到的是，因为产品经理落后太多，工程师完成的工作太多，很多人认为产品经理需要做原型、提交 PR。而你说的，我完全同意，是你的时间最好花在帮助产品经理本身，以及帮助工程师成为更好的产品经理上。这种杠杆作用在大多数情况下远高于你花时间写代码提交 PR。

transcript.secZh: AI 加速下 PM 与设计压力

transcript.sec: Shipping vs. strategy in different company sizes

turns.en: I think that's true in certain circumstances. I think that as a smaller company, I don't know that that's the case. In a smaller company where it's all hands on deck, you probably need to be shipping. If you're in a company where budgets are very tight and engineers are very tight, and you can't just hire because money's unlimited, then you need to do what is needed to accelerate the impact your team is going to have. So there will be a number of cases where as a PM, the right thing to do is to say, screw it, I am shipping, and I am going all the way. But if I'm talking here more about larger companies, more scaled businesses, if you have 20 engineers, is it the highest leverage use of your time to ship an extra feature, or figure out how to up-level everything we are doing, get better user insights, etc.? And I think there's also an element of shipping to learn, building a prototype so that you can have a better opinion. A lot of people talk about just trying three things, seeing how it goes, and that will help inform the roadmap. Now the PRD is to look at it instead of talking about it. So there's a lot of value there still.
turns.zh: 我认为在某些情况下确实如此。但作为一家较小的公司，我不确定情况是否相同。在一个人人全力以赴的小公司里，你可能需要不断交付产品。如果你在一家预算非常紧张、工程师人手不足、无法因为资金无限而随意招聘的公司，那么你需要做必要的事情来加速团队的影响力。因此，在很多情况下，作为产品经理，正确的做法是说：不管了，我要交付，我要坚持到底。但如果我在这里谈论的是更大的公司、更规模化的业务，如果你有 20 名工程师，那么你最高杠杆的时间是利用是交付一个额外功能，还是想办法提升我们正在做的一切，获得更好的用户洞察等？而且我认为还有通过交付来学习的元素，构建一个原型以便你能有更好的判断。很多人说他们只是尝试三件事，看看效果如何，这有助于制定路线图。现在的 PRD 是用来查看的，而不是用来讨论的。所以这仍然很有价值。

turns.en: That's very true. I think that's a great point. So even for me now, where we have a number of PMs and many engineers, there are certain times I want to articulate the idea I have in my head. It's just better for me to prototype it and show it. So I think that is really important. And we are very scrappy. So we are like a big company by name and valuation, but we are extremely scrappy, and the internal focus is just to minimize bureaucracy and just go. So probably 70%, maybe 60-70-80% of what we ship does not have a PRD. I am averse to PRDs. I just hate documentation. I just say go, go, go, cut the blockers. 20-30% of stuff where it's important and really important to get right, the documentation should be really good and people should spend a lot of time on it. But by and large, I think PRDs are outdated at this point, and you can just kick things off with a good team without needing to do that sort of thing.
turns.zh: 这非常正确。我认为这是一个很好的观点。所以即使对我现在来说，我们有很多产品经理和工程师，有时候我想表达我脑海中的想法，对我来说更好的方式就是做一个原型并展示出来。所以我认为这非常重要。而且我们非常灵活。所以我们名义上和估值上是一家大公司，但我们极其灵活，内部的重点就是尽量减少官僚主义，直接行动。所以我们交付的产品中大概 70%，可能 60-70-80% 没有 PRD。我反感 PRD。我讨厌文档。我就是说：做、做、做，砍掉障碍。20-30% 的事情很重要，必须做对，文档应该非常好，人们应该花很多时间在上面。但总的来说，我认为 PRD 现在已经过时了，你可以直接和一个好的团队启动项目，而不需要做那种事情。

turns.en: Say more about that. What do you do to help make sure, because people can build so fast or spend a lot of time going in the wrong direction, ship things that are not what you are asking? How do you kick off a project and clarify here's what we are doing? Is it just a conversation or is there anything beyond that?
turns.zh: 再多说说这个。你如何帮助确保——因为人们可以构建得很快，或者花很多时间走错方向，交付的不是你要求的东西？你如何启动一个项目并明确我们正在做什么？只是对话还是有其他方式？

turns.en: It really depends on the size of it. This goes back to the two-week thing. The reason we have that two-week thing is more like a filter to say if we are investing heavily into something, we should apply more thinking behind it, versus if it's a smaller set of investments, just go for it. As a growth team, you do have a decent amount of these smaller things. So for very small changes, like there's a thing coming, we need to have an upsell for it. What is the thing we are doing? This is just on Slack, purely on Slack. It's just messages back and forth. I think it also depends on having a good caliber of engineer. Engineers can understand, 'Hey, I know you said this, but what about that for the audience?' So we are lucky we have good product-minded engineers in that sense. But all these smaller things are very much just on Slack back and forth, and that's what you do. For the larger things, I very firmly believe in a proper kickoff. There is so much going on at this place. No one has time. No one knows what's going on. So doing a cross-functional kickoff, get legal, get safeguards, get everyone in the room and just say, this is what we plan to do. What do you care about? What do you care about? What do you care about? That 30-minute meeting for larger things is still so important to streamline all the mess that may happen later if you don't do that work early on.
turns.zh: 这真的取决于事情的大小。这又回到了两周的事情。我们之所以有那个两周的事情，更像是一个过滤器，用来判断如果我们对某件事投入很大，我们应该在背后投入更多思考；而如果是一组较小的投入，就直接去做。作为一个增长团队，你确实有很多这样的小事。所以对于非常小的改动，比如某个功能即将上线，我们需要一个升级销售。我们在做什么？这只是在 Slack 上，纯粹在 Slack 上。就是来回发消息。我认为这也取决于工程师的水平。工程师能理解，‘嘿，我知道你说了这个，但那个对用户来说怎么样？’所以我们很幸运，我们有很好的产品思维工程师。但所有这些小事基本上都是在 Slack 上你来我往，这就是你做的。对于更大的事情，我非常坚信要有一个正式的启动会。这个地方事情太多了。没人有时间。没人知道发生了什么。所以做一个跨职能的启动会，让法务、安全保障、所有人都参与进来，然后说：这是我们计划做的。你关心什么？你关心什么？你关心什么？那个 30 分钟的会议对于更大的事情仍然非常重要，可以简化如果你不早期做这些工作后面可能出现的所有混乱。

turns.en: What's your approach to crafting that PRD in those cases? Is it like ramble into Claude? Do you have a template?
turns.zh: 在这些情况下，你如何撰写 PRD？是像对 Claude 随意说一通？你有模板吗？

turns.en: Even in those cases, there are times I don't craft a PRD. Because everything is moving so quickly. So there are still some of those cases where I just set up the meeting. And then 5 minutes before, I'll put it into Coda, like here are some of my thoughts, here are the things I need to think about. Spin up a basic doc and we use it to talk. Other times I won't even have a doc. But if I am creating a PRD, I have basically a skill that I view they've created, and then there are projects with all the previous PRDs in there. It's pretty simple. I have the format down. So I'll just say, here are the things I care about. Here's the why. Here's the problem. And flesh out the key considerations. Flesh out the cross-functional stakeholders, those types of things. But again, my default is if I can avoid the doc and if we can just jump to action, then that's what we should do. And increasingly I just jump to prototyping the thing.
turns.zh: 即使在这些情况下，有时我也不写 PRD。因为一切都在快速变化。所以仍然有一些情况，我只是安排会议。然后在会议前 5 分钟，我会把它放到 Coda 里，比如我的一些想法，我需要考虑的事情。快速创建一个基本文档，我们用这个来讨论。其他时候我甚至没有文档。但如果我要创建 PRD，我基本上有一个技能，我认为他们创建了，然后里面有所有以前的 PRD 项目。这很简单。我有固定的格式。所以我会说：这是我关心的事情。这是为什么。这是问题。然后详细说明关键考虑因素。详细说明跨职能的利益相关者，诸如此类。但同样，我的默认做法是，如果我能避免文档，如果我们能直接行动，那么我们就应该这样做。而且我越来越倾向于直接跳到原型制作。

turns.en: Yeah, that's where I think it'll get so interesting once we can automate more of that. Just like your assistant talking to the legal assistant, ironing out all these little, you know, what's important to you, what's important to the...
turns.zh: 是的，我认为一旦我们能自动化更多这方面的工作，就会变得非常有趣。就像你的助手与法务助手对话，解决所有这些小问题，你知道，什么对你重要，什么对……

turns.en: Yes, and it's coming. And I think the legal has done good enablement around this. We work in these versions of how you can think about how you can mimic what someone might say and set up a quote. So there are things like that that we have now. And then as Claude gets more and more context and gets better at passing long context, I think these are things that Claude will just get better at knowing. One of the most effective ways I use Claude, or one of the most interesting ways I use Claude, is to help you identify misalignment. This is something I have found really, really helpful. So with Coda, you have this Slack MCT.
turns.zh: 是的，而且它正在到来。我认为法务在这方面做了很好的赋能。我们研究这些版本，思考如何模拟某人可能说的话并设置一个引用。所以我们现在有类似的东西。然后随着 Claude 获得越来越多的上下文，并且更擅长传递长上下文，我认为 Claude 会越来越擅长知道这些。我使用 Claude 最有效的方式之一，或者说最有趣的方式之一，是帮助你识别不一致。我发现这真的非常非常有帮助。所以通过 Coda，你有这个 Slack MCT。

transcript.secZh: 不同公司规模的执行与战略

transcript.sec: Using Claude for alignment across Slack

turns.en: Then you can tell Coda to say basically look across Slack, you know the projects that I'm working on, these are the things that are top of mind, go and find me areas of potential misalignment right now. And it does a really, really good job. So, this is something that I have scheduled, runs every week. Claude's like looking at things and coming back to me with and saying like, 'Hey, I think these things you should be aware of.' And so, you can think about it in that shipping context, it's a similar thing where Claude can basically be looking at what's happening across the company and say, 'You're thinking about shipping this thing, here's who you need to talk to, here's what you need to keep in mind.'
turns.zh: 然后你可以告诉 Coda 去查看 Slack，了解我正在做的项目，这些是当前最重要的事情，然后找出潜在的不一致之处。它做得非常好。这是我每周都会安排的任务。Claude 会查看各种信息，然后回来告诉我：‘嘿，我觉得这些事你应该注意。’所以在发货的背景下，类似地，Claude 可以查看公司里发生的事情，然后说：‘你正在考虑发货这个东西，这是你需要沟通的人，这是你需要记住的事情。’

turns.en: That is so cool.
turns.zh: 这太酷了。

transcript.sec: Sponsor message: Vanta

turns.en: I am so excited to tell you about this season's supporting sponsor, Vanta. Vanta helps over 15,000 companies like Cursor, Ramp, Duolingo, Snowflake, and Atlassian earn and prove trust with their customers. Teams are building and shipping products faster than ever thanks to AI. But as a result, the amount of risk being introduced into your product and your business is higher than it's ever been.
