Because things are moving so fast, they are constantly reacting, having to guess at priorities, and having to make do with outdated solutions. Vanta automates compliance and risk management with over 35 security and privacy frameworks, including SOC 2, ISO 27001, and HIPAA. This helps companies get compliant fast and stay compliant. More than ever before, trust has the power to make or break your business. Learn more at vanta.com/lenny. And as a listener of this podcast, you get $1,000 off Vanta. That's vanta.com/lenny.
turns.zh: 我非常兴奋地告诉你本季的赞助商 Vanta。Vanta 帮助超过 15,000 家公司（如 Cursor、Ramp、Duolingo、Snowflake 和 Atlassian）赢得并证明客户的信任。得益于 AI，团队比以往任何时候都更快地构建和发布产品。但因此，产品和业务中引入的风险也比以往任何时候都高。我交谈过的每一位安全负责人都感到保护组织、业务以及客户数据的压力越来越大。因为事情发展太快，他们不断被动应对，不得不猜测优先级，并凑合使用过时的解决方案。Vanta 通过超过 35 个安全和隐私框架（包括 SOC 2、ISO 27001 和 HIPAA）自动化合规和风险管理。这帮助公司快速合规并保持合规。信任比以往任何时候都更能成就或摧毁你的业务。了解更多请访问 vanta.com/lenny。作为本播客的听众，你可以获得 Vanta 的 $1,000 优惠。网址是 vanta.com/lenny。

transcript.sec: AI taking over PM tasks

turns.en: I feel like there's this, what are the jobs of a product manager and then how are they slowly going to be supported or done by AI. So, there's this misalignment, just like fine misalignment, and then maybe one day it'll be like aligning people initially. You talked about this cash automation for growing. Like, how do I grow this product? That's starting to happen. Another that I use myself when I'm building stuff is just asking, how do I make this product better? How do I make this a better user experience? And asking the AI just give me a bunch of ideas, and they're actually really good. So, it's interesting how these little pieces are starting to be put into place to do more and more of this role.
turns.zh: 我觉得这就是产品经理的工作，以及它们如何逐渐被 AI 支持或完成。所以存在这种不一致，就像细微的不一致，然后也许有一天它会像最初对齐人们一样。你谈到了这种用于增长的现金自动化。比如，我如何增长这个产品？这已经开始发生了。另一个我自己在构建东西时使用的就是问：我如何让这个产品更好？我如何让用户体验更好？然后让 AI 给我一堆想法，它们实际上非常好。所以有趣的是，这些小部分开始被整合起来，承担越来越多的这个角色。

turns.en: I'm curious what other automations you have and your team have that are effective. What I love about these conversations is you're basically living in the future. You're working at like the most bleeding edge company with the most talented people, with the most cutting-edge tools, and you can like see where things are going and start to actually live in the future, build things that nobody else has even thought about or can do. I'm curious just what else is working, what else your team has done to help save you time, be more productive.
turns.zh: 我很好奇你和你的团队还有哪些有效的自动化。我喜欢这些对话的地方在于你基本上生活在未来。你在最前沿的公司工作，与最有才华的人一起，使用最先进的工具，你可以看到事物的发展方向，并开始真正生活在未来，构建别人甚至没有想过或做不到的事情。我很好奇还有什么其他有效的方法，你的团队还做了什么来帮你节省时间、提高效率。

turns.en: We use it pretty extensively across the board, right? So, there's the standard PM stuff, like writing docs, brainstorming, looking at data. For data, I personally have like a coworker runs on a schedule and looks at sort of 20 to 25 different charts every morning and then when I come in the morning, there's just so many charts, so many products to track. Cowork will tell me, 'Okay, here are the things that you should pay attention to. Here's like what is concerning and here are just some interesting insights.'
turns.zh: 我们广泛地使用它，对吧？所以有标准的 PM 工作，比如写文档、头脑风暴、查看数据。对于数据，我个人有一个同事按计划运行，每天早上查看大约 20 到 25 个不同的图表，然后我早上来的时候，有太多图表、太多产品需要跟踪。Cowork 会告诉我：‘好的，这些是你应该关注的事情。这些是令人担忧的，还有一些有趣的见解。’

turns.en: And it sends you the update in Slack or what? How does the workflow work?
turns.zh: 它是在 Slack 里给你发送更新吗？工作流程是怎样的？

turns.en: For me it just shows it comes up in Cowork. So Cowork has a schedule desktop app. Yeah, in the desktop app you can have a scheduled task that you create and so I have a bunch of hex links that it will go and look at. It uses the Chrome extension for some things and uses MCP for other things and then it will just give me a summary and then I know, I still there's some few charts that I just like to look at because I'm this guy, I just like charts. So they're all up to the right too. So I like to see the chart but then there's a long tail of things and even the medium tail where it's like you don't have time to look at it every day that if Claude is proactively looking at it and you start to feel good over time of okay, it's like the false positive rate is going down, the false negative rate is going down of things that Claude brings to you then you just get a little bit more confidence and peace of mind there.
turns.zh: 对我来说，它就在 Cowork 里显示。Cowork 有一个桌面应用，你可以创建定时任务，所以我有一堆 hex 链接，它会去查看。它用 Chrome 扩展做某些事，用 MCP 做其他事，然后给我一个摘要。我知道，我还是有一些图表喜欢自己看，因为我就是那种人，我喜欢图表。所以它们都在右边。我喜欢看图表，但还有长尾的东西，甚至中尾的东西，你每天没时间看。如果 Claude 主动查看，随着时间的推移，你会感觉良好，Claude 带给你的误报率在下降，漏报率也在下降，这样你就更有信心和安心了。

transcript.secZh: AI 接管 PM 任务

transcript.sec: Strategy bot and proactive insights

turns.en: This touches on an idea I've always had that teams will have is a strategy bot which just imagine an AI that's constantly watching metrics, the market, the road map, what's working and what's not and just like, 'Hey Amol, here's what I think we should do now. Here's the pivot we should take. Here's where we're going to win.' Like it feels like we're very close to that.
turns.zh: 这触及了我一直以来的一个想法，团队会有一个策略机器人，想象一个 AI 不断观察指标、市场、路线图、什么有效什么无效，然后说：‘嘿 Amol，这是我认为我们现在应该做的。这是我们应该采取的转向。这是我们将要获胜的地方。’感觉我们离那很近了。

turns.en: I think we're close. I think we'll get there later this year to the point where that's very very effective. That's my gut. I think that that level of proactivity and getting looking across a bunch of context and distilling insights, I think it's like the thing I mentioned earlier, Lenny, about the alignment piece. That's like a version of it, right? Now you're just looking at across more data sources.
turns.zh: 我认为我们很接近了。我觉得今年晚些时候我们就能达到那个点，变得非常非常有效。这是我的直觉。我认为那种主动性和跨上下文查看并提炼洞察的能力，就像我之前提到的对齐部分。那是它的一个版本，对吧？现在你只是查看更多的数据源。

transcript.sec: Admin automation and manager lens

turns.en: This is something that I use. So I talked about some of the standard PM stuff with it, you know, brainstorming, data, UXR. There's a lot of admin stuff I just hate, like life admin and paperwork. I just hate it. And so I get Claude to book my meeting rooms. I get him to book meeting rooms. Claude archives my email, first pass at clearing up my inbox. I don't do any of my reimbursements and expenses. Claude will go to Brex and file the reimbursements. He'll go to Brex and file the expenses. So all of that side of things I'm just like just hand it to Cowork. Just get rid of it. And then the stuff that's quite interesting, I think, is the manager lens where I talked about alignment is one thing. So I look also across my direct reports like Claude can look into what have they done this week? You look at our team goals and OKRs. Look at the transcripts from our discussions and understand I can basically ask Claude like what are the key takeaways and observations I should keep in mind and what feedback you think I should give them. And that's something that again, you can just set that up weekly. But the quality is like hit or miss right now. It's like it's decent on some things.
turns.zh: 这是我用的东西。我谈过一些标准的 PM 工作，比如头脑风暴、数据、用户研究。有很多行政事务我讨厌，比如生活行政和文书工作。我讨厌它。所以我让 Claude 帮我订会议室。Claude 归档我的邮件，初步清理收件箱。我不做任何报销和费用。Claude 会去 Brex 提交报销。他会去 Brex 提交费用。所以所有那些事情，我就交给 Cowork。摆脱它们。然后我觉得很有趣的是管理视角，我谈到的对齐是一方面。我也查看我的直接下属，Claude 可以查看他们这周做了什么？查看我们的团队目标和 OKR。查看我们讨论的转录，我基本上可以问 Claude 我应该记住的关键要点和观察，以及你认为我应该给他们什么反馈。这也是你可以每周设置的事情。但质量目前时好时坏。有些方面还不错。

transcript.secZh: 管理自动化与经理视角

transcript.sec: Using AI for Soft Coaching and Feedback

turns.en: Sometimes you're like, "Holy, I am so glad that I caught this." And that's very helpful. And I do that for myself as well. So basically, one of my managers, Amol Avasare, was a podcast guest of yours, right? So I say, "Hey, based on what you know of Amol, both publicly—she's written extensively about product—and internally, and our discussions, what feedback do you have for me as Amol?" And I get that every week. So a lot of these things can already be done today. I think as the models improve, the accuracy and signal of these things will continue to improve rapidly. I don't think you realize how much awesomeness you're sharing here. Every one of these is like, "What?" Okay, so this Amol example. You have one-on-ones with her. You ask Claude to go through all her writing, build a model of her, and ask what you should be doing differently based on what you know about Amol? Is that the difference?
turns.zh: 有时候你会想，“天哪，我真高兴我发现了这个。”这非常有帮助。我自己也这么做。基本上，我的一位经理 Amol Avasare 是你播客的嘉宾，对吧？所以我会说，“嘿，根据你对 Amol 的了解——她公开写了很多关于产品的文章，以及内部讨论——基于我这周所做或未做的一切，你作为 Amol 会给我什么反馈？”我每周都会得到这样的反馈。所以很多事情今天已经可以做到了。我认为随着模型的改进，这些事情的准确性和信号会继续快速提升。我不觉得你意识到你分享的这些有多棒。每一个都让人惊叹。好，所以这个 Amol 的例子。你和她有一对一的会议。你让 Claude 阅读她所有的文章，建立她的模型，然后问根据你对 Amol 的了解，我应该做哪些不同的事情？这就是区别吗？

turns.en: Yeah, effectively. There are a number of ways to do this. If someone has a public profile where they've written extensively, it's helpful because Claude can get all that information. Otherwise, you can have a project or a skill, but increasingly Claude is better at understanding because you can tell Claude, "Look on Cowork." You can say, "Using the Slack MCP, look at everything this person has said in the last week, etc. Based on that, what are the top priorities? What are my manager's priorities based on how they're spending their time that I don't know?" All this layer of stuff, I think about it as soft coaching. I think that is unlocked in my opinion already. It's like working with a coach who's kind of drunk at times—not drunk—but sometimes says something and you're like, "Why would you bring that up? That's clearly wrong." But other times you're like, "Wow." There's one of the guys, Scott, who leads our enterprise team. There were a few cases where he found major areas of misalignment that would have caused teams to spin their wheels significantly or do overlapping work. You think about the impact of that: shipping in this world, in a bigger company, is often constrained by cross-functional coordination. I think we're now starting to see at that cross-functional coordination layer that AI can really be used to reduce that toil. Six months ago that wasn't possible, and I wonder what will be possible six months from now.
turns.zh: 是的，基本上是这样。有很多方法可以做到这一点。如果某人有公开资料，写了很多文章，那很有帮助，因为 Claude 可以获取所有信息。否则，你可以创建一个项目或技能，但 Claude 越来越擅长理解，因为你可以告诉 Claude，“在 Cowork 上查一下。”你可以说，“使用 Slack MCP，查看这个人上周说的所有内容，等等。基于此，最重要的优先事项是什么？我的经理根据他们的时间安排有哪些我不知道的优先事项？”所有这些层面，我把它看作是软性指导。我认为这已经可以实现了。就像和一个有时有点醉醺醺的教练一起工作——不是醉——但有时他说了些什么，你会想，“你为什么要提这个？这明显是错的。”但其他时候你会想，“哇。”我们企业团队的负责人 Scott 有几次发现了重大的对齐问题，这些问题本会导致团队白费力气或做重复工作。想想影响：在这个世界里，在一家大公司，交付往往受到跨职能协调的限制。我认为我们现在开始看到，在跨职能协调层面，AI 确实可以用来减少这种辛劳。六个月前这还不可能，我不知道六个月后可能实现什么。

turns.en: And the fact that all this data is there—Slack, Granola, or whatever people use—all these notes from conversations and discussions are really key to this working.
turns.zh: 所有这些数据都在那里——Slack、Granola 或人们使用的任何工具——所有这些来自对话和讨论的笔记确实是让这一切发挥作用的关键。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: So for someone who wants to do something like this, how do you set it up? What are the steps?
turns.zh: 那么对于想要做类似事情的人，如何设置？步骤是什么？

turns.en: Go on Cowork, download the desktop app on Cowork. Connect the Slack MCP. Depending on how big the organization is, you may need team or enterprise admin permissions to enable that. But once you have the Slack MCP connected on Cowork, you just ask Claude. That's it.
turns.zh: 去 Cowork，下载 Cowork 的桌面应用。连接 Slack MCP。根据组织的大小，你可能需要团队或企业管理员权限来启用它。但一旦你在 Cowork 上连接了 Slack MCP，你只需要问 Claude 就行。就这么简单。

turns.en: Amazing. Okay, I'm going to go in a different direction. I'm going to talk about the focus that Anthropic has had over the years. If you look at the numbers you're putting up, what's really incredible is the focus you've had. I think this is the reason it has worked out so well. There's a certain competitor in the market that is realizing they should have done this and are starting to shift to a similar approach. As an external observer, it feels like Anthropic has been very good at doing very few things but going super deep. B2B, for example, just going deep on B2B and then going really deep on coding use cases. Claude Code being an example. And it's worked out really well. Who has been driving that focus? Who has helped keep that focus from the beginning?
turns.zh: 太棒了。好的，我要换个方向。我想谈谈 Anthropic 多年来一直保持的专注。看看你们取得的数字，真正令人难以置信的是你们的专注。我认为这是它如此成功的原因。市场上某个竞争对手正在意识到他们本应这样做，并开始转向类似的方法。作为一个外部观察者，感觉 Anthropic 非常擅长做很少的事情，但做得非常深入。例如 B2B，只是深入 B2B，然后深入编码用例。Claude Code 就是一个例子。而且效果非常好。是谁在推动这种专注？是谁从一开始就帮助保持了这种专注？

turns.en: I think it's a foundational part of the company. It's been there from the very early days, and it really comes from leadership. They've done a phenomenal job of distilling that. I saw a doc come up recently—it was something Dan Mad, one of our founders, had written. It's dated 2021, a few months after they started the company. It was like, "Here's why we should just focus on AI coding." This is five years ago, long before anyone knew the actual market opportunities around this. I think this is a deep focus we have had internally from the start on the importance of coding and B2B. There are two lenses: a view that this could be commercially beneficial to tackle, and also the side of accelerating research. It's a pretty mainstream view now—I've heard people from various labs talking about the importance of coding to accelerate research. But that has been a very firm view we have been laser-focused on internally: if you have the best models, that's going to accelerate your research loop. I think that's something Dario has seen very clearly for a number of years. So I would say a lot comes from Dario, from leadership, and from our DNA. The second is probably necessity. It's now changed—we're a more well-known company, raised lots of money, etc.—but historically we were the smallest, least well-funded player in this space. In many ways, it's a complete miracle that we've gotten to the stage we have. We didn't have the free cash flow or distribution of Meta or Google, nor the first-mover advantage of OpenAI.
turns.zh: 我认为这是公司的基础部分。从很早开始就存在了，而且真正来自领导层。他们在提炼这一点上做得非常出色。我最近看到一份文档——是我们的一位创始人 Dan Mad 写的。日期是 2021 年，公司成立几个月后。内容大概是，“为什么我们应该专注于 AI 编码。”这是五年前，远在任何人知道实际市场机会之前。我认为这是我们内部从一开始就对编码和 B2B 重要性的深度专注。有两个视角：一个是这可能在商业上有利可图，另一个是加速研究。现在这已经是一个相当主流的观点——我听到各个实验室的人都在谈论编码对加速研究的重要性。但这是我们内部一直非常坚定的观点：如果你有最好的模型，那将加速你的研究循环。我认为 Dario 多年来一直非常清楚地看到了这一点。所以我会说很多来自 Dario，来自领导层，来自我们的 DNA。第二个可能是必要性。现在情况变了——我们是一家更知名的公司，筹集了很多资金等等——但历史上，我们是这个领域最小、资金最少的参与者。在很多方面，我们能走到今天这一步完全是个奇迹。我们没有 Meta 或 Google 的自由现金流或分发渠道，也没有 OpenAI 的先发优势。

transcript.sec: Freedom through constraints and Anthropic's early days

turns.en: And so like what do you do, right? I think there's this broader principle I have just around life of freedom through constraints. When you have a bunch of constraints applied on you, whether that's in personal life or at work, I think that can bring a lot of freedom because it just frees up all this excess choice. You're like, okay, this is clearly the path. And I think for us it's like, okay, you don't have a ton of funding, you're a small player, you don't have distribution. You just have to really pick a very narrow focus and even for a very generalizable technology to maximize your chances of getting to escape velocity. And I think that's also relating to how history played out, right? So, it was well before my time, but Anthropic had a version of Claude. We had a chatbot before ChatGPT was launched. And we had ultimately chosen not to launch it for safety reasons. I think the team didn't want to kick off effectively an AI global arms race. And ChatGPT launched and they got insane traction, right? And that just naturally sort of pulled them towards consumer. There's another world where if Anthropic had launched Claude first, maybe it did the other way around even with all that focus stuff. So, who knows? It's hard to say looking back at some of these things.
turns.zh: 所以，你该怎么办呢？我认为生活中有一个更广泛的原则——通过约束获得自由。当你身上有很多约束时，无论是个人生活还是工作，我认为这能带来很多自由，因为它释放了所有多余的选择。你会想，好吧，这显然是该走的路。对我们来说也是如此：你没有大量资金，你是小玩家，没有分销渠道。你只能选择一个非常狭窄的焦点，即使对于一项非常通用的技术，也要最大化你达到逃逸速度的机会。我认为这也与历史如何展开有关，对吧？所以，那远在我入职之前，但 Anthropic 有一个 Claude 的版本。我们在 ChatGPT 发布之前就有一个聊天机器人。但我们最终出于安全原因选择不发布它。我认为团队不想引发一场全球 AI 军备竞赛。而 ChatGPT 发布了，他们获得了惊人的增长，对吧？这自然就把他们拉向了消费者方向。在另一个世界里，如果 Anthropic 先发布了 Claude，也许情况会反过来，即使有那些专注的因素。所以，谁知道呢？回顾这些事情总是很难说。

turns.en: Wow. I didn't know that. I think also people just don't realize how far behind Anthropic was. Like right now it's like, of course they're amazing. But I just remember when people were talking about Anthropic raising money. I was like, there's no way they're going to compete with OpenAI at this point. It's like so over. They're so far ahead. And it shows the power of focus and just all the things you all did. It is absurd how far you all have come and how successful and how things have changed so quickly.
turns.zh: 哇，我不知道这个。我认为人们也没有意识到 Anthropic 当时落后了多少。现在当然觉得他们很棒。但我记得当人们谈论 Anthropic 融资时，我想，他们不可能在这一点上与 OpenAI 竞争。已经结束了。他们领先太多了。而这显示了专注的力量，以及你们所做的一切。你们取得的进步、成功和变化之快，简直不可思议。

turns.en: Yeah. And I would say I very much agree. I think a lot of that comes down to our leadership team. We have a number of people who've worked at what I call the best companies in the world. Very senior people. And I think almost uniformly everyone's like this is the strongest leadership team out of any of these companies. So I think a lot comes from them and then we're just very lucky to have incredible people. I think that helps a lot as well.
turns.zh: 是的，我非常同意。我认为这很大程度上归功于我们的领导团队。我们有很多人曾在所谓的世界最佳公司工作过，都是非常资深的人。几乎所有人都一致认为，这是所有公司中最强的领导团队。所以我认为很多都来自他们，而且我们很幸运拥有优秀的人才，这也很有帮助。

turns.en: On the coding piece just to make sure that part is clear. transcript.sec: Balancing growth with AI safety mission

turns.en: The safety piece is really interesting. So I'll spend a little time here. So famously Anthropic, I think the official name of Anthropic is Anthropic an AI safety research company. As a growth person there's this balance I imagine you strike between growing and the mission being don't grow at all costs. Our goal is AI safety and alignment. How do you balance those two things? How does that impact your job?
turns.zh: 安全部分真的很有趣。所以我花点时间在这里。众所周知，Anthropic 的官方名称是 Anthropic 一家 AI 安全研究公司。作为一个负责增长的人，我想你在增长和使命之间取得平衡，使命是不计一切代价地增长。我们的目标是 AI 安全和对齐。你如何平衡这两件事？这对你的工作有什么影响？

turns.en: Look, I think it's something we take very very seriously and it is the whole reason the company exists. And if you think about it, it's all the way from why they left to start the company. It's deep in our corporate structure itself. So, historically everyone raising money is like go create a Delaware C-corp and that's the structure you do. And with a corporation, you have a fiduciary duty to maximize returns to shareholders, maximize shareholder value. And from the beginning they went a different way. We created a public benefit corporation PBC, which allows you to legally say that maximizing shareholder value is not the overarching umbrella goal of this company. And you can optimize for public benefit. So, really I think it starts from there and it ladders down from there. For us, our purpose, our mission ultimately is to make sure that the transition to powerful AI goes well and is net beneficial for humanity. We are very excited, honestly, very optimistic about where this can go. But we also understand what the risks are. And so for us that top line objective of this just needs to go well for humanity is something that we are happy to take a significant commercial hit for. And we've done that time and time again, right? So, back then we had Claude, but we didn't want to release it because there was a safety risk. And so time and time again I've seen we've been happy to take that hit and it's actually worked out well for us in other ways. From a growth lens, I look at it as growth teams can often push the boundaries of what is good user experience etc., because they're trying to eke out metrics at times. When I think about if a controversial test is brought to me, I sort of look at it as two types of tests that are controversial. One is when that test is so controversial that you just should not run it because the results don't matter because you would not ship it for a combination of brand, customer friendliness, and values. And the second is where it's controversial, yikes, I don't like it, I certainly don't love it, but it's not a red line. So if someone comes to you with conviction and says I have a really good hypothesis around this, and you're like I don't love it but you can run the test and see what impact it has. And if it's a high level of cringe or yikes, then I want to see a high level of return for the result. I kind of think about everything in terms of is it in one or is it in two, and for every company that one and two is different. I think for us the AI safety is very much in one. It's why we exist. We're just not going to do this thing.
turns.zh: 听着，我认为我们非常非常重视这一点，这也是公司存在的全部原因。如果你仔细想想，从他们离开创办公司的原因开始，这一点就深深植根于我们的公司结构中。所以，历史上每个人融资时都会说，去创建一个特拉华州 C 型公司，这就是你要做的结构。而对于一家公司，你有信托责任去最大化股东回报，最大化股东价值。但从一开始，他们就走了一条不同的路。我们创建了一家公益公司（PBC），这让你可以合法地说，最大化股东价值并不是这家公司的首要目标。你可以为公共利益而优化。所以，我认为这从那里开始，并层层向下。对我们来说，我们的目标、我们的使命最终是确保向强大 AI 的过渡顺利进行，并对人类产生净收益。我们非常兴奋，老实说，对它的前景非常乐观。但我们也理解风险所在。所以对我们来说，这个首要目标——这必须对人类有利——是我们愿意为此承受重大商业打击的。而且我们一次又一次地这样做，对吧？所以，当时我们有 Claude，但我们不想发布它，因为存在安全风险。所以我一次又一次地看到，我们乐于承受这种打击，而且实际上它在其他方面对我们有利。从增长的角度来看，我认为增长团队常常会推动良好用户体验等的边界，因为他们有时会试图挤出指标。当我考虑是否要做一个有争议的测试时，我把它看作两种类型的有争议测试。第一种是测试如此有争议，以至于你根本不应该运行它，因为结果不重要，你不会因为品牌、客户友好度和价值观的综合原因而发布它。第二种是它有争议，哎呀，我不喜欢它，我当然不爱它，但它不是红线。所以如果有人带着信念来找你，说我对这个有一个非常好的假设，而你说我不喜欢它，但你可以运行测试，看看它有什么影响。如果它让人非常尴尬或糟糕，那么我希望看到结果有很高的回报。我基本上把所有事情都归为第一类或第二类，对每家公司来说，第一类和第二类是不同的。我认为对我们来说，AI 安全绝对属于第一类。这是我们存在的理由。我们就是不会做这件事。

transcript.secZh: 平衡增长与 AI 安全使命

transcript.sec: Leaving money on the table for long-term growth

turns.en: I think then there's other things that fall into two where you're like heightened sensitivity but we can try it and see what happens. I think zooming out, Lenny, one of the biggest mistakes I feel like I see growth teams make, and particularly just hardcore growth practitioners, is just trying to squeeze every last dollar. This is like a general principle in life too: if you're a founder raising money, just trying to squeeze out the last dollar, you don't want to do that because you want people to come back next time as well, in my view. And I think in growth, it's really important that you usually need to be okay leaving money on the table. That's a core principle for us as a growth team: we are very comfortable forgoing metric impact in order to prioritize safety, our brand, hold a high-quality bar, and maintain a great user experience. If you look beyond the short-term and ask, 'What are the numbers for this quarter?' and zoom out and think about what the very best products out there are, you realize this is how they all operate. And that's actually the thing that's going to drive more growth long-term as well. I think that ties back to safety: as the risks get higher and the stakes get higher, the fact that we are taking a stance on safety is critical to what we do and is actually going to become a significant competitive advantage for us that will help us in the long run.
turns.zh: 我觉得还有一些事情属于第二种情况，就是高度敏感，但我们可以试试看会发生什么。放大来看，Lenny，我觉得我看到增长团队，尤其是那些硬核增长实践者，犯的最大错误之一就是试图榨干每一分钱。这其实也是生活中的一个普遍原则：如果你是一个创始人，在融资时试图榨干最后一分钱，你其实不应该这么做，因为你希望别人下次还会回来，这是我的看法。我认为在增长中，你通常需要接受“留点钱在桌上”这一点，这非常重要。这是我们增长团队的一个核心原则：我们非常愿意为了优先考虑安全、品牌、保持高质量标准和维护出色的用户体验而放弃指标上的影响。如果你超越短期，问自己“这个季度的数字是多少？”，然后放大来看，想想那些最优秀的产品，你会发现它们都是这样运作的。而这实际上也是推动长期增长的关键。我认为这又回到了安全问题上：随着风险和利害关系越来越高，我们在安全上采取立场对我们所做的事情至关重要，而且实际上会成为我们一个显著的竞争优势，长期来看会帮助我们。

turns.en: Yeah, and as you share all this, clearly it's working and Anthropic is killing it. So I'm loving those examples when someone approaches a problem that way, it actually works out. It's the same way I think about with my newsletter. There's so much more I can do to grow it. My philosophy is just focus on creating good content, nothing else. All these micro-optimizations are not going to matter in the end. It'll grow through people sharing it if it's useful to them. So on a very small scale, I have a similar philosophy.
turns.zh: 是的，当你分享这些时，很明显它奏效了，Anthropic 做得非常出色。所以我喜欢那些例子，当有人用这种方式处理问题时，它真的能成功。这和我对我 newsletter 的想法是一样的。我可以做更多事情来增长它，但我的理念就是专注于创作好内容，其他什么都不管。所有这些微优化最终都不重要。如果内容对人们有用，它会通过分享来增长。所以在很小的规模上，我有类似的理念。

transcript.secZh: 为长期增长放弃短期收益

transcript.sec: Advice for thriving in the AI future

turns.en: One of the things people are probably thinking about as we talk. You know, we joke about AI replacing parts of jobs here and there. It is pretty scary to a lot of people. Just like, 'What is the future of my job? Will I have a job? How do I stay relevant in this future?' So there are a couple questions here. One is just for folks that want to thrive in this approaching AI future as a PM, as a growth person, do you have any advice, things that they should be doing right now?
turns.zh: 在我们谈话时，人们可能正在想一件事。你知道，我们开玩笑说 AI 正在取代部分工作。这对很多人来说相当可怕。就像，‘我工作的未来是什么？我会有工作吗？我如何在这个未来中保持相关性？’所以这里有几个问题。一个是，对于那些想在即将到来的 AI 未来中茁壮成长的产品经理、增长人员，你有什么建议，他们现在应该做什么？

turns.en: To me, a couple of things come up. One thing that everyone says is use the tools. You need to be on top of the tools. I think you need to be using Claude Code, you need to be using Cohere and understanding each model release, what new things you can do with this, how you can apply this to your job. It'll work well in some things, it'll work terribly in other things, and then one model later it's like, 'Oh that other thing worked.' But if you didn't go back to try it, you would not have known, and now many months have passed and you didn't know that was possible. So being on top of the tools is really important both for improving your own productivity, but also for getting product sense around AI products, which I think is just going to become increasingly important. I think then, zooming out beyond that, I kind of look at it as leaning into where you have a competitive advantage and an unfair advantage. So to me, it's like: there are some PMs who are really good at craft, for example, and there are others who you'll throw them into a situation with all these stakeholders who have strong opinions, and there's no way they're going to mediate this and they come out and everyone's kind of swimming in the right direction. So if you think about what is the major skill set you have where you spike that can be tied to delivering impact for a company in a product role, I would just double down on that and almost forget the weaknesses. Just what can you do to become the best person at that thing? Because that's very valuable, and I think that ties to the notion of leaning into being interdisciplinary. Going back to some of what we mentioned earlier: in this world where engineers are mini PMs, the engineer who is highly product-brained is a unicorn, an absolute unicorn. I think the same thing is true for PMs: if now the designer is really stretched and you're a PM who can design, you are also a unicorn. The chances of a company letting you go have gone down dramatically because you are now so much more useful. So I think that is really important. I think if I look at what's benefited me, it's probably a version of this. I came from a founder background, so the mix of founder background, finance as an investment banker, and then sales—I almost became an account exec instead of going into product. I was right on the edge of should I go into sales or product? I think a combination of those along with growth is probably what's led to certain areas and situations where I can have outsized impact compared to other people. Understanding what that is for you is really important. I look at our financial services product. We've launched Quip for Sheets, Quip for Excel. They're going to take the market by the way, everyone when Quip launched. I know. But the guy running that, Nick Lin, he came from investment banking, he came from private equity. And he just has such a competitive advantage where he's building that product and he's like, 'I know this. I know this.' He's built for this. So I think just understanding what those interdisciplinary areas are that you can lean into to make yourself higher impact is really important. And then the last one is just being adaptable. Anyone who is trying to just keep applying old playbooks, I think you're going to make life a lot harder for yourself. One of the biggest things when you come into Anthropic is you need to understand that probably 50, 60, 70% of how you operated in the past, just throw it out the door.
turns.zh: 对我来说，有几件事。第一件事是每个人都在说的：使用工具。你需要掌握这些工具。我认为你需要使用 Claude Code，你需要使用 Cohere，并理解每个模型发布后你能用它做什么新事情，如何将其应用到你的工作中。它在某些事情上会表现很好，在其他事情上会很糟糕，然后一个模型之后，你会说，‘哦，那件事现在可以了。’但如果你没有回去尝试，你就不会知道，而几个月过去了，你都不知道那是可能的。所以掌握工具非常重要，既能提高你自己的生产力，也能培养对 AI 产品的产品直觉，我认为这只会变得越来越重要。然后，放大来看，我倾向于把它看作是利用你拥有竞争优势和不公平优势的地方。所以对我来说，就像：有些产品经理非常擅长工艺，而另一些产品经理，你把他们扔进一个充满各种利益相关者、每个人都有强烈意见的情况中，他们无法调解，但最终每个人都能朝着正确的方向前进。所以如果你想想你最大的技能集是什么，你在哪里特别突出，并且这可以转化为在产品角色中为公司带来影响，我会加倍投入在那上面，几乎忽略弱点。就专注于你能做什么来成为那方面最好的人？因为那非常有价值，而且我认为这与跨学科的理念有关。回到我们之前提到的：在这个工程师是迷你产品经理的世界里，一个高度产品思维的工程师是独角兽，绝对的独角兽。我认为产品经理也是如此：如果设计师非常紧张，而你是一个能设计的产品经理，你也是独角兽。公司解雇你的可能性大大降低，因为你现在更有用了。所以我认为这非常重要。如果我看是什么让我受益，可能就是这个版本。我有创始人背景，所以创始人背景、投资银行的金融背景，然后是销售——我差点成为客户经理而不是进入产品。我当时就在纠结是去销售还是产品？我认为这些组合加上增长，可能导致了在某些领域和情况下，我能比其他人产生更大的影响。理解对你来说那是什么非常重要。看看我们的金融服务产品。我们推出了 Quip for Sheets、Quip for Excel。它们会占领市场，顺便说一句，当 Quip 推出时，每个人都知道。但负责那个产品的人，Nick Lin，他来自投资银行，来自私募股权。他就有这样的竞争优势，他在构建那个产品时，就像‘我懂这个。我懂这个。’他就是为此而生的。所以我认为理解你可以利用哪些跨学科领域来让自己产生更大影响非常重要。最后一点就是保持适应性。任何试图继续应用旧剧本的人，我认为你会让自己的生活更加艰难。当你来到 Anthropic 时，最重要的事情之一就是你需要明白，过去你运作方式的 50%、60%、70% 可能都要扔掉。

transcript.sec: Adaptability and Deep Expertise

turns.en: It's not going to be relevant. And if you try to stick to that, you're going to have a lot of friction and it's not going to be helpful. So just being adaptable and understanding that okay, the job's changed this way. I'm going to go that way is I think it's so so important. That is awesome advice. It matches a lot of what Jenny shared when she came on the podcast, the design leader on Claude and Claude work and all these things. Oh, just like the idea of going deep, becoming the best or one of the best at a very specific thing. And that not every company will need and you don't need all 10, but just going deep on something. I forget what how she described it. Mark Andreessen said the same thing. Just like I think what we call it a sideways E instead of just like T-shaped or one thing. If you could have a couple things you're really really good at. There's a lot of power to that.
turns.zh: 那不会相关。如果你坚持那样做，你会遇到很多摩擦，而且不会有帮助。所以保持适应，理解工作已经变了方向，我就朝那个方向走，我认为这非常重要。这是很棒的忠告。这和 Jenny 在播客中分享的很多内容一致，她是 Claude 和 Claude 工作的设计负责人。哦，就像深入钻研、成为某个非常具体领域的最佳或最佳之一的想法。并不是每家公司都需要，你也不需要全部十项，但深入某件事。我忘了她是怎么描述的。Marc Andreessen 也说过同样的话。就像我们称之为“侧向 E”而不是 T 型或单一技能。如果你能有两三件非常擅长的事，那会很有力量。

transcript.sec: Anthropic's Culture and People

turns.en: Oh man. Okay. Before I get into something that I think will blow a lot of people's minds about how we actually met and kind of a big part of your journey. Let me just ask you this. Is there anything else about Anthropic that might be worth sharing, might be worth talking about?
turns.zh: 哦，天哪。好的。在我谈到一件我认为会让很多人震惊的事情——我们实际上是如何认识的以及你旅程中很重要的一部分之前，让我先问你这个问题。关于 Anthropic，还有什么值得分享或谈论的吗？

turns.en: The thing that maybe comes to mind is just like our culture and the people that we have here. I really think it's our secret sauce. I think it's the thing that is the most defensible, the thing that no one else is going to be able to replicate. And I don't think it's an accident. Like leadership has really invested in this a lot and Daniela and Dario, they really believe in this a lot and I think it's just created a very special culture. So, I would say that, you know, this is like truly a mission-driven company. And I was not 100% sure of that when I joined. Like I was like I think that this is an exciting company. I very much agree with their principles, but I didn't know anyone at the company. I didn't have any references on what it was like inside. So, I was a little skeptical when I joined. I'm like, at least they're talking about it, but like I don't know. Are they serious about this? And then I came in very early. I was like, oh okay, like they are maybe they're even more serious about this internally than they talk externally. And so, it's just like it's a mission-driven company where people viscerally understand both the upsides and the downsides of the technology and therefore understand the divergent ranges of how this may go for humanity and how different of a future that could be. And I think when you understand how different this could be as a future for all of us and like our children and our grandchildren, etc. I think it leads to a lot of passion for what we do and it just leads to a lot of belief in what we're doing and so I kind of look at every other job I've had in the past, there's some degree of people at the company who are just checked out where it's like, you know, I'm here. I'm sick of this but I don't have a better option or I'm getting paid too much to leave, etc. That is just not the case here. I have not met a single person — I'm saying I'm really not a single person who's checked out. Everyone is putting everything they have on the table. Everyone is pouring it out and leaving nothing behind and is just fully in it. And so I think that leads to this releases energy that is just very hard to describe. I think you have that energy, you have that mission-driven nature and then it's such an open culture. So leadership is very transparent with us on things. We Slack is like a it's a whole maze. Every There are so many things that play out in Slack. We're very open. Everyone has these notebook channels where you kind of have your own like Twitter feed in a way where you're just talking about your thoughts about things and so you can go and join the Slack channel the notebook channels of people on research and all these other areas and you'll you can learn whatever you want and you can spend so much time getting lost in that as well. But that openness where we even encourage like people can just argue with Dario there was an all-hands he said something where someone didn't agree and then the person goes into Dario's notebook channel and just says, "Hey, I didn't appreciate how you said this and this and that" and then it sparked a whole big debate but that sort of thing where it's encouraged like go to leadership and disagree with them, challenge them publicly and I think that just leads to a level of trust and all of that together I think it just means that we have this very deep sense of togetherness that man I have just never I've never experienced anything like it and then you get to the talent, right? That I think that is the thing where the talent density is like I feel like I'm playing for Real Madrid at times. I look around and I'm like man I'm playing for Madrid where it's like you just have the best people in the world. I think it's most the case on research. We have the very best researchers in the world but even you look on product like Amie Vora she is phenomenal. We have Mike Krieger you're like okay casually started Instagram he's here you know on growth we have Johnny again who is my engineering counterpart he's the OG in growth engineering he's great. We have Alexa who's like guy teaches growth engineering at Reforge he's just another dude on the team and all of that I think is just very special. My favorite here is like in the linear a couple of months ago we had our on-site company-wide on-site in October and I'm walking around and I see this guy he's just walking around eating popcorn by himself. I go up to him and I'm like you're Jeff, right? And he's like I am and I'm like you are literally the US ambassador to my country Australia and you're just an employee here. I'm like this is insane and talking to you about prime ministers of our country and all these things and it's just the talent combined with that culture I think is this secret sauce that is the reason that I think we are as successful as we are.
turns.zh: 可能想到的是我们的文化和这里的人。我真的认为这是我们的秘方。我认为这是最难以被复制的东西，是别人无法复制的。而且我不认为这是偶然的。领导层在这方面投入了很多，Daniela 和 Dario 非常相信这一点，我认为这创造了一种非常特殊的文化。所以，我想说，这是一家真正使命驱动的公司。我加入时并不完全确定。我当时想，这是一家令人兴奋的公司。我非常同意他们的原则，但我不认识公司里的任何人。我对内部情况没有任何参考。所以我加入时有点怀疑。我想，至少他们在谈论这个，但我不知道他们是否认真。然后我很早就进来了。我意识到，哦，他们内部可能比对外说的更认真。所以，这是一家使命驱动的公司，人们发自内心地理解这项技术的好处和坏处，因此理解这对人类可能产生的不同走向以及未来可能有多么不同。我认为当你理解这对我们所有人、我们的孩子和孙辈的未来可能有多么不同时，它会对我们所做的事情产生巨大的热情，并带来巨大的信念。所以我回顾过去的所有工作，公司里总有一些人只是混日子，比如“我在这里，我厌倦了，但我没有更好的选择，或者我薪水太高离不开”等等。这里完全不是这样。我没有遇到一个人——我真的没有遇到一个混日子的人。每个人都全力以赴，倾尽所有，完全投入其中。我认为这释放了一种难以描述的能量。你有这种能量，有这种使命驱动的本质，还有如此开放的文化。领导层对我们非常透明。Slack 就像一个迷宫。很多事情都在 Slack 上发生。我们非常开放。每个人都有笔记本频道，有点像你自己的 Twitter 信息流，你在上面谈论你的想法，所以你可以加入研究人员的笔记本频道，学习任何你想学的东西，也可以花很多时间沉浸其中。这种开放性甚至鼓励人们与 Dario 争论。有一次全体会议，他说了一些话，有人不同意，然后那个人就进入 Dario 的笔记本频道说：“嘿，我不喜欢你这样说，等等”，然后引发了一场大辩论。但这种鼓励人们去领导层那里提出异议、公开挑战他们的做法，我认为这带来了信任。所有这些加在一起，意味着我们有非常深厚的团结感，我从未经历过类似的事情。然后还有人才，对吧？我认为人才密度很高，有时我感觉自己像是在为皇家马德里踢球。我环顾四周，心想，天哪，我在为皇马踢球，这里有世界上最好的人。我认为在研究方面尤其如此。我们有世界上最好的研究人员，但即使在产品方面，像 Amie Vora，她非常出色。我们有 Mike Krieger，他随便就创办了 Instagram，他在这里。在增长方面，我们有 Johnny，他是我的工程对应方，他是增长工程领域的 OG，他很棒。我们有 Alexa，他在 Reforge 教增长工程，他只是团队里的另一个人。所有这些都非常特别。我最喜欢的是，几个月前，在十月份的全公司现场活动中，我四处走动，看到一个家伙独自吃着爆米花。我走过去说：“你是 Jeff，对吧？”他说：“是的。”我说：“你实际上是美国驻澳大利亚大使，而你只是这里的一名员工。”我说这太疯狂了，和你谈论我们国家的总理等等。我认为人才加上这种文化就是秘方，也是我们如此成功的原因。

transcript.sec: Scaling beliefs and views across a growing organization

turns.en: It's a way where people share provocative things. I think from a leadership level, we also think about it as a way to scale your beliefs and views across an org as it grows quickly. If you're adding a lot of people, you need to think about what behaviors we want to model and what principles are top of mind. You can have meetings and model behavior, but if you have a channel where you share your top of mind, like a post I did about the importance of being comfortable leaving money on the table, new engineers see that and realize it's different from what they knew before. So it's good for openness, and also for a leader to scale their views and get people up to speed, which is important to avoid drift. With many new people, drift can lead to strategy and perception drift. It helps run a tighter ship.
turns.zh: 这是一种人们分享挑衅性观点的方式。从领导层面来看，我们也认为这是在组织快速扩张时，将你的信念和观点规模化传播的方式。如果你在增加很多人，你需要思考我们想要树立什么样的行为，以及哪些原则是最重要的。你可以开会，也可以以身作则，但如果你有一个渠道来分享你的首要想法，比如我上周发的一篇关于“乐于把钱留在桌上”的重要性的帖子，新加入的工程师看到后就会意识到，这和他们以前知道的不一样。所以这对开放性有好处，对领导者来说也是将自己的观点规模化、让团队快速了解做事方式的好方法，这对于避免漂移非常重要。当有很多新人加入时，漂移会导致战略和认知上的偏差。这有助于更严格地管理公司。

turns.en: And more importantly, it's data for the agents that run, to help them work with all these humans.
turns.zh: 更重要的是，这些内容为运行的智能体提供了数据，帮助它们与所有人类协作。

turns.en: Yes, that is very correct. It goes to Claude. There are certain documents in onboarding where the HR team has written, 'Before editing anything on this document, please check with this person because this is a document that Claude references as a key thing.' So more and more, these types of things—how the growth team thinks, how safeguards think—you're arming Claude with that context to get better at helping in the future.
turns.zh: 是的，非常正确。这些内容会进入 Claude。在入职文档中，HR 团队会注明：“在编辑此文档前，请先与某人确认，因为这是 Claude 引用的关键文档。”所以越来越多这类事情——增长团队怎么想，安全团队怎么想——你是在用这些上下文武装 Claude，让它未来能更好地提供帮助。

turns.en: That's interesting. It feels like that's something every company will have to start doing: sharing their thoughts in a structured way so that all these agents we have running have what they need to know.
turns.zh: 这很有意思。感觉每家公司都得开始这样做：以结构化的方式分享想法，这样我们运行的所有智能体都能获得它们需要知道的信息。

transcript.sec: Anthropic's stance on AI risks and optimism

turns.en: Just coming back to the values thing, I want to highlight something important. A lot of people criticize Anthropic for talking about dangers to humanity, throwing out numbers about jobs going away, creating fear. People think we're trying to raise money or get attention or create headlines. But everything I've seen internally is always just: this is what we believe, we want people to know what might be coming. Even if it isn't bad, we want people to understand what might happen and we are trying to avoid it. You might ask, why is Anthropic even building AI if it's so dangerous? Ben shared the understanding that we think it's better to go at this and try to build it the right way rather than just hope nothing bad happens.
turns.zh: 回到价值观这件事，我想强调一个重要的点。很多人批评 Anthropic 谈论对人类的危险，抛出关于工作岗位消失的数字，制造恐惧。人们认为我们是在试图筹钱、吸引注意力或者制造头条新闻。但我内部看到的一切始终是：这是我们的信念，我们希望人们知道可能会发生什么。即使结果不坏，我们也希望人们了解可能发生的情况，并且我们正在努力避免它。你可能会问，既然这么危险，为什么 Anthropic 还要构建 AI？Ben 分享的理解是，我们认为与其什么都不做、指望坏事不会发生，不如主动应对并尝试以正确的方式构建它。

turns.en: Yeah, three things come to mind. First, I go back to something I said earlier: we think about things from an exponential lens. If you think from a linear lens, you see the world very differently. You look at where we are today and ask, how much better can it be in two or three years? If you look from an exponential lens and understand how exponentials work, you realize that a lot of this stuff will happen sooner than people think if they're looking from a linear lens. And if you understand that there could be upsides and downsides and a range of outcomes, you need to talk about the downsides to avoid them and push towards the upsides. Most people at the company are optimists; we're very optimistic about the future. But we understand the risk—it's not guaranteed that we end up in a good place, and not enough people are talking about the risks in productive ways, especially those who have the know-how. People may talk about risks but aren't in the game, so they don't fully know the specific risks. We're in the game, we understand what's happening. Second, I think we actually believe in this stuff more strongly than we say externally. It's such a key part of how we think internally that sometimes we need to reword statements because people might think we're being too over the top, but internally that's how we think. What we put out is a softer version.
turns.zh: 嗯，我想到三点。第一，回到我之前说的：我们以指数视角看待事物。如果你以线性视角看，你会看到完全不同的世界。你看看今天的情况，然后问，两三年后能好多少？如果你以指数视角看，并理解指数增长如何运作，你就会意识到，很多事情的到来会比人们用线性视角想象的要快。如果你明白可能有正面和负面结果以及一系列可能性，你就需要讨论负面结果以避免它们，并推动正面结果。公司里大多数人都是乐观主义者；我们对未来非常乐观。但我们理解风险——我们并不保证最终会处于一个好的境地，而且没有足够多的人以富有成效的方式讨论风险，尤其是那些了解风险内情的人。有些人可能谈论风险，但他们不在局中，所以并不完全了解具体的风险。我们在局中，我们了解正在发生的事情。第二，我认为我们实际上比对外表达的更坚信这些。这在我们内部思考中是非常关键的一部分，以至于有时我们需要重新措辞，因为人们可能觉得我们太夸张了，但内部我们就是这样想的。我们对外发布的是经过软化的版本。

transcript.secZh: Anthropic 对 AI 风险与乐观立场

transcript.sec: Influence through participation

turns.en: Um and then I I think the the third piece is that is is going to what Ben said, where we we think a lot about driving the race to the top. And so it's like if you're not in the game and you're shouting from the sidelines, no one cares. It's just the reality of of how the world works. Like no one no one really cares. Those two I consistently recommend to people. And then the third one is more relevant to product in many ways: Thinking in Bets by Annie Duke. I think just being able to break down a situation and someone says, 'I don't know if it'll get done in time.' Okay, can you put a number to that? What percentage likelihood is it? Those types of things are so tactically helpful in product.
turns.zh: 这可能和我刚才聊的很多内容有关。我经常思考冥想和我的情绪状态，这是我在工作之外花最多时间思考、阅读、研究和努力的事情。所以我的推荐也与此相关。第一本是佛教僧侣咏给·明就仁波切写的《喜悦之道》。这本书讲的是如何以不同的方式看待你的生活经历，并提供了一些策略来改变你对事物的看法。我向很多人推荐过这本书，他们都非常喜欢。另一本是安东尼·德·梅洛的《觉醒》，内容类似但角度不同。这两本书我一直向别人推荐。第三本在某种程度上与产品更相关：安妮·杜克的《对赌思维》。我认为能够分解一个情境，当有人说‘我不知道能不能按时完成’时，你可以说‘好的，你能给个数字吗？大概有多大可能性？’这类事情在产品工作中非常有战术价值。

transcript.sec: Favorite Recent Movie or TV Show

turns.en: Favorite recent movie or TV show?
turns.zh: 最近最喜欢的电影或电视剧是什么？

turns.en: I think movie is probably Mardi Gras Supreme. It's one of the only ones I watched recently. I thought it was great. I thought it was just insane, absolutely insane movie. It was ridiculous but I loved it. TV show I have not watched TV in a while. I think it's probably the Olympics if that counts.
turns.zh: 电影的话，可能是《狂欢节至尊》。这是我最近看的为数不多的电影之一。我觉得很棒，简直疯狂，绝对疯狂的电影。虽然很荒谬，但我很喜欢。电视剧我很久没看了。如果算的话，可能是奥运会。

transcript.sec: Favorite Product Discovery

turns.en: Is there a product that you love, like something you discovered recently or just have in your life that's like, 'Oh, this is very cool. People might want to know about it.'
turns.zh: 有没有你很喜欢的产品，比如最近发现的或者生活中一直在用的，让你觉得‘哦，这很酷，人们可能想知道它’？

turns.en: Yeah, so this is a funny one. I was in Japan last week and I was sitting in this hotel and I just really loved the pillow that I had in this hotel. It was very random. I've had at times neck and upper trap pain and sometimes I've been frustrated with my pillow because I sometimes sleep facing up, sometimes sideways and the height's not quite right. And I was just like this pillow is just great. It's full of beads so you can naturally shift the height of the pillow while you're sleeping, even unconsciously without thinking. So I looked at the tag, I was like what is this? I ordered it on Amazon Japan and brought it back with me on the plane. I have the name here: it's called the Maruhachi Shinsui Maruhachi Pro Pillow. You can only get it in Japan but they ship to the US. No affiliation. I think that has changed my life in the last week.
turns.zh: 嗯，这有点好笑。我上周在日本，住在酒店里，非常喜欢酒店里的那个枕头。这很偶然。我有时脖子和上斜方肌会疼，而且因为有时仰睡有时侧睡，枕头高度总不合适，让我很烦恼。但这个枕头就很好。它里面装满了珠子，所以你可以在睡觉时自然地调整枕头高度，甚至无意识地就能做到。所以我看了标签，心想这是什么？我在亚马逊日本站下单了，然后带上飞机带回来了。名字在这里：叫 Maruhachi Shinsui Maruhachi Pro Pillow。只有日本有卖，但他们可以寄到美国。我没有利益关系。我觉得这枕头在过去一周改变了我的生活。

transcript.sec: Favorite Life Motto

turns.en: Do you have a favorite life motto that you often come back to in work or in life?
turns.zh: 你有没有一个在工作和生活中经常想起的人生格言？

turns.en: Main one I'd say is just 'she'll be right.' This is a very common Aussie saying. It's like 'she will be right.' When we are faced with a tough or difficult situation, you're kind of dismissing the severity of it by saying 'yeah, it'll be fine.' I think that's such a good tactic in many cases. And then the other one is 'sometimes in life you just have to go for it.' That is something that Eros Resmini, who was the CMO of Discord and a very close advisor to me as a founder, would push me on. He would often say 'just go for it.' I think it's very helpful: you can sit there thinking 'should I do this, should I not,' and sometimes in life you just have to go for it.
turns.zh: 最主要的一个是‘她会没事的’。这是澳大利亚很常见的说法，就是‘她会没事的’。当我们面临困难或棘手的情况时，你通过说‘是的，会好的’来淡化它的严重性。我认为在很多情况下这是一个很好的策略。另一个是‘有时候在生活中你只需要放手去做’。这是 Discord 的前 CMO Eros Resmini 经常推动我的，他是我作为创始人的非常亲密的顾问。他经常说‘放手去做’。我觉得这非常有帮助：你可以坐在那里想‘我该不该做’，但有时候在生活中你只需要放手去做。

transcript.sec: New Hobby

turns.en: I'm curious, you used to be into martial arts, that didn't go great. Do you have a new hobby that you find yourself loving? Do you make time for hobbies?
turns.zh: 我很好奇，你以前喜欢武术，但不太顺利。你现在有没有新的爱好？你会为爱好腾出时间吗？

turns.en: I think I'm just quite tight on time for hobbies. I would say it's largely just work and have my body function and then take care of the most important relationships around me. I think maybe the one hobby I'd say is just I'm really into sports and I've gotten more into football in particular, watching, but certainly not playing. My wife is from Michigan and she took me to a game at the Big House and it just changed my life. From then on I was like, I'm a Wolverine fan. Big Wolverine fan, big 49ers fan. I just love football, so that's probably the one that comes to mind.
turns.zh: 我觉得我的时间很紧，没什么时间给爱好。基本上就是工作、保持身体机能，然后照顾身边最重要的关系。我想唯一的爱好就是我很喜欢体育，尤其是橄榄球，看球但不打球。我妻子来自密歇根，她带我去 Big House 看了一场球赛，那改变了我的人生。从那以后我就成了狼獾队的粉丝。我是狼獾队的大粉丝，也是 49 人队的大粉丝。我就是喜欢橄榄球，所以这大概就是我能想到的爱好。

transcript.sec: Closing and Where to Find Amol

turns.en: Amol, this was incredible on so many levels. I'm so thankful you made time for this considering how much you have going on. Where can folks find you online and say they want to apply for a job maybe on your team or where can they find that? And how can listeners be useful to you?
turns.zh: Amol，这次访谈在很多层面上都太棒了。非常感谢你在百忙之中抽出时间。大家可以在网上哪里找到你？如果他们想申请你团队的工作，应该去哪里？听众怎样才能帮到你？

turns.en: You can find me on my LinkedIn. It's just Amol Avasare. I'm boring. That's it. You can look online to apply on our jobs page. It's roles across growth engineering, growth product, growth design. We are looking for great people on the growth team, so please come and join us. And being helpful to me is just two things: trying our products, giving us feedback, harsh feedback in particular on what we can do better, and please send great people our way. We are looking for the best of the best to join the team and we would love to hear from anyone you know who could be a fit for one of our roles.
turns.zh: 你可以在 LinkedIn 上找到我，就是 Amol Avasare。我很无趣，就这样。你也可以在我们的招聘页面上在线申请。我们有增长工程、增长产品、增长设计等职位。我们正在为增长团队寻找优秀人才，所以请加入我们。至于如何帮我，只有两件事：试用我们的产品，给我们反馈，尤其是严厉的反馈，告诉我们哪里可以做得更好；另外，请把优秀的人才推荐给我们。我们正在寻找最优秀的人加入团队，如果你认识适合我们某个职位的人，我们很乐意听到他们的消息。

turns.en: Dream job for so many people. Amol, thank you so much for being here.
turns.zh: 对很多人来说这是梦想的工作。Amol，非常感谢你来做客。

turns.en: Thanks, Lenny. Bye, everyone.
turns.zh: 谢谢，Lenny。大家再见。

turns.en: Thank you so much for listening. If you found this valuable, you can subscribe to the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify or your favorite podcast app. Also, please consider giving us a rating or leaving a review as that really helps other listeners find the podcast. You can find all past episodes or learn more about the show at lennyspodcast.com. See you in the next episode.
turns.zh: 非常感谢你的收听。如果你觉得本期内容有价值，可以在 Apple Podcasts、Spotify 或你喜欢的播客应用上订阅本节目。另外，请考虑给我们评分或留下评论，这真的能帮助其他听众找到这个播客。你可以在 lennyspodcast.com 找到所有往期节目或了解更多信息。下期再见。
